The word means 'nearest/closest' and refers to this temporary worldly life. The ٱ is a special way of writing the 'al' (the) when connecting words.

steps.m: to curse or drive away from mercy

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed the middle vowel to sukun and added ة to make it a noun meaning 'one instance of cursing'

steps.m: a curse (object of أُتْبِعُوا/they were followed by)
steps.n: Added tanween (ً) to show this is the object being acted upon by the verb أُتْبِعُوا (they were followed by)
Connected the letters with vowel marks to make the basic noun 'day'
The fatha at the end shows this word is connected to what comes after it (القيامة/resurrection), making it mean 'the day of resurrection'
Adding وَ at the beginning connects this to what came before it, like saying 'and'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Changed the middle letter و to ي and added alif to make it a noun meaning the action itself
Added ة to make it mean a specific type of rising - the resurrection
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and changed the alif to a long alif
Added kasra at the end to show this word comes after يَوْمَ (day) in the phrase 'Day of Resurrection'
This is a basic attention-getting particle made up of two parts: أَ (which asks for attention) and لَا (which emphasizes). It's used here to draw attention to the curse that follows.
The alif at the end is written as a long alif (آ) to show emphasis in writing, but the meaning stays the same. This is just a writing style choice.
Added kasra (ِ) under the first letter to show correct pronunciation
Added shadda (ّ) to emphasize the meaning. This makes the statement that follows more certain and important.

steps.m: to return or come back repeatedly

steps.n: Adding ا makes it a past tense verb
Adds tanween alif to show this word is receiving the action of being cursed in this ayah

steps.m: to reject, deny, or cover up truth

steps.n: Added vowel marks to make it a past tense verb showing one person did this action
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action together, and changed the last vowel to ُ to match with وا. The small mark after وا (۟) is just to show proper pronunciation.

steps.m: to raise, nurture, develop, care for
Lord (one who nurtures and cares completely)
steps.n: The shadda shows this is someone who nurtures and cares in a complete way, like a master caretaker

steps.m: their Lord (the one who nurtures and cares for them)
steps.n: Adding هُم shows this Lord belongs to or is connected to them
This letter starts questions or draws attention to what follows

steps.m: Now behold! (used to draw attention to what follows)
steps.n: Adding لَا makes the attention-drawing stronger and more emphatic. This combination is used to introduce important statements, especially warnings or significant announcements.
This root carries the basic meaning of distance or being far away
Changed from a basic root meaning 'far' into a noun meaning 'distance' by adding the vowels u and silence mark
Added tanween fatha to make it an accusative noun used as a curse, similar to saying 'away with them!'
The laam is strengthened with shaddah (ّ) and tanween kasra (ٍ) is added to mark the genitive case, as this is a diptote proper noun

steps.m: to return repeatedly, to come back often
steps.n: The root becomes the name of an ancient Arabian tribe
Adding alif makes this the name of the tribe
Changes the tribal name 'Aad' to show it belongs to or relates to something

steps.m: surely for Aad (emphatic, referring to the tribe's punishment)
steps.n: Adds emphatic preposition لِ with shadda (ّ) meaning 'surely for/to'. This emphasis connects to the context of the ayah which discusses the punishment of Aad.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create a noun meaning a group of people
Added kasra at the end to show these people belong to Prophet Hud. This is like saying "Hud's people" in English.

steps.m: to guide, lead (original meaning of root before becoming proper name)

steps.n: Adding damma makes it into a proper name
Adding the two dots below (tanween) shows this is 'of Hud' - like saying it belongs to or is about Hud
This is a basic preposition showing direction or destination. In this ayah, it connects to the word ثَمُودَ (Thamud) to show who the message was sent to.
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
Added the small alif (ٰ) at the end to show this is how the word is written in the Quran. This doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: Thamud (the name of an ancient Arab tribe)
steps.n: This is a proper noun referring to an ancient Arab tribe mentioned in the Quran
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of the word إِلَىٰ (to) that comes before it in the ayah. This means the message was sent 'to Thamud'.
Three letter root أ-خ-و combined with a fatha (short 'a' sound) on the first letter to make the basic word for 'brother'
Added alif at the end to show this is the object being acted upon
Added هُمْ at the end to show this brother belongs to them (referring to the people of Thamud)
Changes the action into someone who does that action. Like changing 'fix' to 'fixer' in English.

steps.m: a righteous one (object of implied 'We sent')
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is being acted upon by an implied verb 'We sent' that isn't written but is understood from context. Like saying '[We sent] a righteous one.'
Added fathas to make basic past tense form
Changed قَوَلَ to قَالَ because و changes to ا in hollow verbs

steps.m: to stand together, gather, rise up

steps.n: The root meaning of standing/gathering together becomes a word for a group of people who gather as one
Added يٰ at the start to call out to the people, like saying 'Hey people!' in English
Added kasra at the end to show possession - like saying 'my people' instead of just 'people'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb
Changes from past tense 'he worshipped' to a direct command meaning 'you worship!' by adding alif at start and changing vowels

steps.m: worship! (command to all of you) (connected to قَالَ يٰقَوْمِ - he said: O my people)
steps.n: Added plural marker to make the command address multiple people. This matches the context where Salih is addressing his entire people.
Changed to the proper name Allah by adding ال and merging letters. This is a unique word in Arabic that always refers to The One True God.

steps.m: Allah (as the object being worshipped in اعْبُدُوا)
steps.n: The word 'Allah' gets a special mark (shadda) on the ل and a small mark (fatha) at the end to show it's receiving the action of worship
This is a negative particle that negates the word 'god' (إِلٰهٍ) in this ayah. It's part of the phrase 'you have no god' (مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلٰهٍ)
Starting with the basic preposition ل and adding fatha to help with pronunciation
Added كم which means 'you all' to show who the preposition is directed to
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere else. The kasra (ِ) and sukun (ْ) help us pronounce it correctly.

The shadda (ّ) appears because this word comes after a word ending in ن. When this happens, the two ن sounds merge together and we write a shadda. This word connects to أَنشَأَكُم (He created you) to show what you were created from.

steps.m: to worship or be devoted to

steps.n: Changes from root meaning into noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
The alif is written as a small mark above to show the long 'aa' sound

steps.m: any deity (in مَا لَكُم مِّنْ إِلٰهٍ - you have no deity)
steps.n: Added tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this is any deity in general, not a specific one. This word is part of the phrase 'you have no deity' in the ayah.

steps.m: to change, to be different from

steps.n: The root 'change/differ' becomes 'other than' by adding vowels. The ُ (damma) at the end shows this word is doing something in the sentence
Added هُۥ at the end to mean 'Him', referring back to Allah

steps.m: He (referring to Allah in this ayah)
steps.n: This is a standalone pronoun that refers to someone being discussed. In this ayah, it refers to Allah who was mentioned earlier when Salih told his people to worship Him.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb

steps.m: to cause to grow, to create, to originate
steps.n: Adding أَ at the beginning makes the verb causative - changing 'come into existence' to 'bring into existence/create'. The first root letter gets a sukun (ْ).

steps.m: He created you all (Allah is the subject)
steps.n: Added كُم at the end to show that 'you all' are what was created. This refers to Allah creating humanity.
This is a basic particle that shows something comes from somewhere or is part of something
The shadda (ّ) is added because the next word starts with ال, making the ن sound stronger
The fatha (َ) is added at the end to help connect this word smoothly to the next word الْأَرْضِ (the earth)
Added vowel marks to show how to say 'earth/land' - the short 'a' sound (َ) and silent ر make it a word for a real thing you can touch
The ِ (kasra) at the end shows this word follows مِنَ (from) in the ayah
Adding ال makes it refer to the specific earth/land rather than any earth/land. This word follows مِنَ (from) in the phrase مِنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ (from the earth)

steps.m: to build, develop, or make something prosperous

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past-tense verb
The prefix است changes the meaning to 'seeking to do' the action
Added كُمْ to show that 'you all' are the ones being made to build/develop

steps.m: and He made you all build/develop (referring to Allah)
steps.n: Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement about Allah's creation
Basic preposition meaning 'in' or 'within'
Added ها at the end to show this means 'in it' where 'it' refers to something feminine (in this case, referring back to الْأَرْضِ meaning 'the earth')
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added ٱسْتَ at the beginning to show seeking or requesting the action
Changed vowels to make it present tense 'I' form
Added وا at the end to change from 'I seek' to 'you all seek!' (command to a group)

steps.m: seek forgiveness from Him! (command to many people)
steps.n: Added هُ to show who forgiveness should be sought from

steps.m: so seek forgiveness from Him! (command to many people)
steps.n: Added فَ to connect this command with previous statement
This is a connecting word that shows one thing happens after another. Here it connects 'ask His forgiveness' with 'turn back to Him' in the ayah.

steps.m: to return or turn back (to what is right)

steps.n: Basic command form telling one person to return/repent
Added وا to make it a command for multiple people
Added ا to complete the plural command form
This is the basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'towards'. It's used to show direction or destination.
The ى changes to ي with sukun to prepare for connecting with the pronoun. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added هِ at the end which means 'him'. In this ayah, it refers back to Allah who was mentioned earlier (اللَّهَ).
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it stronger. In this ayah, it emphasizes that 'my Lord is near and responsive.'

steps.m: to nurture, care for, or be master of

steps.n: Root letters combined with fatha and doubled middle letter to make the word for 'lord'
Added kasra to prepare for possession
Added ى to show the lord belongs to me. This word is described by قَرِيبٌ مُّجِيبٌ (near and responsive) in this ayah.
Added vowels to make an adjective describing a quality
Added ي to make it stronger - like changing 'near' to 'very near' or 'close by'
Added tanween (double damma) to show this is any nearby thing, not a specific one
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Added مُ at the start and changed the middle vowels to show who does the action. The و changed to ي because it comes after a kasra sound.
Added two damma marks at the end to make it indefinite (tanween)
Adding short vowels 'a' after each letter to make 'he said' in past tense
Changed middle letter و to ا and added وا at the end to show many people did the action
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changes from 'he fixed' to 'one who fixes/righteous person' by adding alif after first letter. Turns a verb into a noun describing someone who does that action.

The يٰ at the start means 'O' (calling out to someone). The word صٰلِحُ is Prophet Salih's name, which comes from the word meaning 'righteous'. When we call out to someone in Arabic, we add يٰ at the start.
This is a particle that adds emphasis or shows something is completed. Here it comes before كُنتَ (you were) to emphasize that something was definitely true in the past.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense form
The word got shorter because the middle letter و dropped out, making it a command 'Be!'
Added تَ to show this is talking about 'you' in the past. This word is part of what Salih's people said to him.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'in' or 'within'

steps.m: in us (object of كُنتَ - you were)
steps.n: Added نا at the end to show this means 'in us'. This word connects to كُنتَ (you were) earlier in the verse, showing who was being hoped in.

steps.m: to hope, expect, or look forward to

steps.n: Added مَ at the beginning to make it 'the one who is hoped for' (like changing 'hope' to 'hoped for')

steps.m: hoped for (as the object being described)
steps.n: Added ًّا at the end to show this word is describing what's being affected by the hoping
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters. This creates the basic noun meaning 'before'.
Added fatha at the end because it connects to the word after it (مَرْجُوًّا) to show what happened before
This is the basic demonstrative word meaning 'this'
Added هٰ at the beginning to show we're pointing to something nearby
Changed the final alif to an elongated alif (آ). This is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added ت at start to show 'you' are doing the action, and changed vowels to show it's happening now
Added أَ at start to make it a question
Added ىٰ to show the long 'aa' sound at the end
Added نَآ at the end to show that 'us' is being stopped/prevented

steps.m: that (introduces what they were forbidden from: نَّعْبُدَ - worshipping)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that introduces what comes next in the sentence. Here it shows what the people were forbidden from doing - worshipping what their fathers worshipped.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense 'we' form by adding ن at start and changing vowels
Added shadda on ن and changed final vowel to fatha because it comes after أَن in the ayah
This is a relative pronoun meaning 'what' or 'that which'. In this ayah, it refers to what their fathers used to worship, connecting to يَعْبُدُ (worship) in the next part.
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this is a past-tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, sukun on ب, and damma on د. This word refers to their worship of what their ancestors worshipped, showing their resistance to change.
Root meaning 'father' changes to make many fathers (plural) - like changing from 'dad' to 'dads'
Adds hamza at the end because the word ends with a long alif
The word 'fathers' gets a waaw (و) at the end to show it's doing the action (like saying 'the fathers did' instead of just 'fathers')
Adds 'our' to show possession
Starting with the emphatic particle إِنَّ which adds certainty to what follows
Adding نَا shows this is about "we/us"
Adding وَ connects this to what came before it in the sentence
The preposition فِي is written with ى instead of ي at the end. This is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: surely in (expressing doubt about ما تَدْعُونَآ)
steps.n: Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis. In this ayah, it's used with إِنَّنَا to express that they are definitely in doubt about what Salih is calling them to.
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger
Added the genitive case ending because this word comes after the preposition فِى (in) in the ayah
Starting with the basic preposition that shows where something comes from
Added ما which makes it ask about or refer to something general, like saying 'what' or 'that which'

steps.m: from what (that you call us to)
steps.n: When ن meets م, the ن blends into the م making it stronger (shown by the ّ mark). This word refers to what Salih was calling his people to.
Added vowels to connect the letters. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added ت at start to show 'you' are doing it. Changed vowels to show it's happening now. The و at the end is from the root.
Added نَآ at the end to show 'us' as the ones being called. The آ makes the sound longer.
Basic preposition meaning direction or movement towards something
The ى changes to ي with sukun when connecting to pronouns
Added the pronoun ه with kasra to show 'him'
Added مُ at the start and ِ in the middle to show this thing causes doubt in others. This word describes the doubt (شَكٍّ) mentioned in the ayah.

steps.m: causing doubt (describes شَكٍّ/doubt, in a state of being owned/possessed)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra at the end to show this word is describing something owned or possessed. It's describing the doubt (شَكٍّ) that the people are in.
Basic root letters ق-و-ل got vowels (َ) to make it 'he said' in the past
The و changes to ا because it's a weak letter
Changes from verb 'to stand' into noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Added يٰ at the beginning to make it a call or address to people

steps.m: O my people! (subject of قَالَ - he said)
steps.n: Added ِ at the end to show possession - these are 'my' people. This word comes after قَالَ (he said) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters and added vowels to make the basic past tense form
Changed to 'you all' form by adding تُمْ at the end
Added أَ at the start to turn 'you all saw' into 'have you all seen?' - makes it a question word

steps.m: if (introduces condition for عَصَيْتُهُۥ - I disobey Him)
steps.n: This is a conditional particle that introduces a hypothetical situation. In this ayah, it connects the condition 'if I disobey Him' to what would happen in that case.
The weak letter و drops out and the vowels change according to regular rules for hollow verbs (verbs with و in the middle)
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did this action in the past
This is a basic preposition that shows something is on top of or over something else. It doesn't come from a root.
Added fatha marks to help with pronunciation. The first fatha goes on ع and the second on ل.
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show it's an alif maksura, which is how this word is written in the Quran.
Added vowel marks and doubled the middle letter to show intensity
Added ة to make it a feminine noun meaning 'clear evidence'
Added tanween kasra to show it comes after the word عَلَىٰ (upon) in the ayah
This is a basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere else
The shadda (ّ) appears because this word comes after a pause in speech. It connects to the word رَّبِّى (my Lord) to show 'from my Lord'

steps.m: to care for and help grow
one who cares for and helps grow
steps.n: Connected the letters and doubled the middle letter to show this describes someone who does this action perfectly
Added ي at the end to show it's mine, and ِ to connect it smoothly
Added emphasis to the first letter because it comes after a pause in speech
Changed the root to Form IV pattern to show causing someone else to receive something. The ى at the end is because this is a weak verb.
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) to show this is the end of the word
Added نِى at the end to show the action was done to 'me'

steps.m: and he gave me/brought to me
steps.n: Added وَ at beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added هُ at the end to show who is being referred to. This connects to the previous word رَّبِّى (my Lord) in the ayah, showing what Salih is receiving his evidence from.

steps.m: to show kindness and care for someone

steps.n: Changed from basic root letters meaning 'to be kind' into a noun meaning 'kindness' by adding the vowel marks َ (fatha) and ْ (sukun)
Adding ة makes it refer to one specific instance

steps.m: a specific act of kindness (object of آتَانِي - He gave me)
steps.n: Adding ً shows this is what was given (the object)

steps.m: who (asking about يَنصُرُنِى - will help me)
steps.n: This is an interrogative pronoun asking about who will help. It connects to the verb يَنصُرُنِى (will help me) that comes after it in the ayah.

steps.m: so who (asking about يَنصُرُنِى - will help me)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this question follows from what was said before. It's like saying 'so' or 'then' in English when asking a follow-up question.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels
Added نِى at the end to show the help is being given to 'me'
This is a preposition that shows something comes from somewhere else or is part of something else
Added fatha at the end to connect it smoothly to the next word (اللَّهِ). This combination means 'from Allah'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding vowel patterns
Added 'al' to make it specific/definite - like changing 'a god' to 'the god'
Added kasra at the end to show it means 'of the god'
This is a primitive particle (حرف شرط) that has no root or derivational forms. It is not derived from any root.
Connected the letters and added vowel marks. The final ي changes to ى in past tense.
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action. The ى changes back to يْ when adding endings.
Added هُ to show who was disobeyed. The ۥ mark shows this is pronounced with a long vowel.
Basic interrogative particle meaning 'what'

steps.m: so what (connects to يَنصُرُنِى - will help me)
steps.n: Added فَ at beginning to show this question follows from the previous statement about who will help
Connected the root letters with basic vowels
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' are doing the action
Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action
Added نِى at the end to show the action is being done to 'me'

steps.m: to change or be different from something

steps.n: Basic noun form showing something being different or other than something else

steps.m: other than (object of تَزِيدُونَنِى - you increase me)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is receiving the action of the verb تَزِيدُونَنِى (you increase me)
Added vowels (fatha) to make it 'he lost' in the past tense
Doubling the middle letter changes it from 'he lost' to 'he made someone lose'
Changed to a noun form that describes the action itself

steps.m: a causing of loss (object of غَيْرَ)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show this is indefinite and is the object of the word غَيْرَ (other than) in this ayah
Added vowel marks to connect letters. The root meaning of standing together became associated with a group that stands as one.
Adding يٰ at the start makes it a call or address to someone
Adding ِ at the end shows possession (my people)
Adding وَ at the start connects this phrase to what came before it
Basic demonstrative word meaning 'this'
Added ذا to make it specifically for pointing to things
Changed ending to هِ to make it feminine, and added ِ (kasra) to show it's pointing to something specific
Added small ي (ۦ) as a pronunciation helper. This word is pointing to the she-camel (نَاقَةُ) mentioned right after it in the ayah.
Root letters joined together with vowels to make a noun meaning 'excellence', especially when describing camels
Changed from a root word about camels to mean 'one female camel' by adding ا in the middle and ة at the end to make it feminine and singular

steps.m: the female camel (subject of تَأْكُلْ - she eats)
steps.n: Added damma to show this is the subject doing the eating in the verse
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One God. The hamza is dropped and replaced with ٱ at the start.
Added kasra at the end to show this describes whose punishment it is (عَذَابٌ/punishment belongs to Allah)
Added fatha to the letter ل to show its pronunciation. This particle shows purpose or direction.
Added كُمْ which means 'you all' to show who the action is for. This word connects to نَاقَةُ (she-camel) in the ayah, showing the she-camel is for them.
The hamza changes to ء and the first ي becomes ا due to weak letter rules
Adding ة makes it into a noun meaning one specific instance of showing/being clear

steps.m: a sign (object of فَذَرُوهَا - so leave her)
steps.n: Adding tanween fatha (ً) shows this is the object being acted upon in the sentence. It connects to the verb فَذَرُوهَا (so leave her) where 'sign' is what's being left.
The initial و is dropped in the command form. Added damma to make it a command.
Added و to make the command plural - addressing multiple people
Added ها to show the command is about leaving 'her' (referring to the she-camel mentioned as نَاقَةُ ٱللَّهِ in the previous verse)
Added فَ to connect this command with the previous statement about the she-camel being a sign
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb
Changed from past tense 'he ate' to present tense 'you eat' by adding تَ at start and changing middle vowel

steps.m: in (connects أَرْضِ to what comes before)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows something is located inside or within something else. In this ayah, it connects 'land/earth' (أَرْضِ) to the previous context.

steps.m: in (connects أَرْضِ to what comes before)
steps.n: The ي is written as ى with a small alif (ٓ) on top when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters together with fatha on first letter and sukun on second letter to form the basic noun.
The ِ ending shows this word belongs to Allah in this ayah - it's Allah's earth. This construction is called 'possession' in Arabic.
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Added genitive case ending to show possession/belonging
This is a connecting particle that joins this phrase to what came before it
Added fatha to show how the word is pronounced
Added لا which makes this a negative command. This connects to the verb تَمَسُّوهَا (touch it) to say 'and do not touch it'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form basic past tense verb
Changes 'he touched' to 'you touch' repeatedly/strongly by adding تَ and doubling middle letter
Makes verb plural masculine 'you all'

steps.m: you all touch her (referring to نَاقَةُ ٱللَّهِ - Allah's she-camel)
steps.n: Adds feminine object pronoun 'her' referring to Allah's she-camel mentioned in the ayah
Changed the root into a noun meaning the harmful or evil thing itself

steps.m: with harm (object of تَمَسُّوهَا - touch her)
steps.n: Added بِ at beginning to mean 'with' and ٍ at end to show it's affected by the word before it. This describes what they were not supposed to do to the camel.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and adjusting vowels
Added فَ to show this is a consequence of previous action (disobeying the warning)

steps.m: and then he will seize you all (as punishment)
steps.n: Added كُمْ to show who is being seized (the people being warned)
Added alif after second letter to change from action to result. This word describes what will happen to them.
Added tanween dammah (ٌ) to show this is the subject doing the action of taking/seizing in يَأْخُذَكُمْ
Added vowels and ي to make an adjective describing the quality of being near
Added tanween dammah to make the word indefinite
Added fatha after each letter to make past tense verb meaning 'he slaughtered'
Added و and damma to make 'they' form of the verb
Added هَا to show the female camel is being acted upon
Added فَ to show this action follows something before it in the story
Past tense verb meaning 'they slaughtered' with 'ha' at end referring to female camel
The middle letter و changes to ا when adding past tense vowels - this is a common pattern for hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in the middle)

steps.m: then he said (after عَقَرُوهَا/they slaughtered her)
steps.n: The فَ shows this saying happened right after they slaughtered the camel mentioned in the previous word عَقَرُوهَا
Added basic vowels to connect the letters and make a past tense verb
The doubled letter shows doing something a lot or with intensity
Adding ت shows doing the action to yourself (reflexive)
وا suffix makes it a command to a group (like saying 'all of you enjoy!')
This is a fundamental preposition particle that shows location or being inside something. It is not derived from a root.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: to circle, revolve, or move around

steps.n: Adding the fatha vowel mark shows this is a past tense verb
Added kasra at the end to show this word connects to something coming after it
Added كُمْ at the end to show the home belongs to multiple people being spoken to
Adding basic vowel marks to make the word readable
Adding ا makes it a specific number word
Added ة at the end to make it a countable number (like when counting 1,2,3) instead of just meaning 'three' in general
Changed alif to alif maqsurah and added fatha to show it's the object of 'enjoy' (تَمَتَّعُوا)
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic word for a single day
Changed from singular 'day' to plural 'days' by adding أَ at the start and putting a double mark (shadda) on the middle letter
Added tanwin kasra to show this word is being counted (as part of ثَلٰثَةَ أَيَّامٍ meaning 'three days')
The basic demonstrative pronoun ذا (this) gets an alif above it to show distance
Added لِ to extend the meaning and show more distance
Added كَ at the end to show we're pointing to something specific in this ayah - in this case, pointing to the warning/promise mentioned
Root letters turned into a noun meaning 'promise' by adding vowel marks (fatha and sukoon)
Added tanween (double damma) to make it an indefinite noun
Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create a noun meaning 'other than'
Added damma (u sound) at the end because this word is doing the action - it's telling us what is 'not false'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he lied' to 'something that was lied about' by adding مَ at start and و after ذ - like changing from 'write' to 'written'
Added the tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end because this word is describing وَعْدٌ (promise) in the ayah, saying it's 'a promise not lied about'
This is a basic particle meaning 'when' that shows something happened right after something else. Not derived from a root.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens right after something else, connecting it to what happened before.
Added vowel marks (fathas) to the root letters to make it mean 'he came' in the past tense
Changed to hollow verb pattern by adding alif after first letter
Extended the alif to show emphasis/intensity of coming
Changed from basic root letters to a noun by adding marks (fatha and sukun), making it mean 'a command' instead of 'to command'
Added damma to show this word is doing the action in the sentence - it's the thing that 'came' (جَآءَ)
Added نَا to show the command belongs to us. This whole word is what 'came' in the sentence.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The doubled ج shows doing something with extra effort or intensity
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action
Added alif after first letter to show who does the action, like changing 'write' to 'writer' in English

steps.m: a righteous one (being saved by نَجَّيْنَا)
steps.n: Added ًا (tanween) at the end to make it mean 'a righteous person' - this shows Salih is the object (the one receiving the action) in the sentence
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that refers to someone or something
Adding ن at the end makes it refer to multiple people instead of just one
Adding ال makes it more specific, like saying 'those who' instead of just 'who'
Adding و at the beginning connects this word to what came before it, like saying 'and'
Added alif after first letter to strengthen the meaning from just trusting to actively believing
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word correctly
Added ه which means 'him', referring back to Prophet Salih who was mentioned earlier in the verse. The ۥ mark is just to show proper pronunciation.

steps.m: to be merciful, to show compassion
steps.n: This root is about being kind and merciful
Added ة to make it into a specific instance of mercy, like one merciful act. Added َ (fatha) on first letter and ْ (sukun) on middle letter to show it's a noun.

steps.m: by/with a mercy (connected to نَجَّيْنَا - We saved)
steps.n: Added بِ at start to mean 'with/by' and ٍ (tanween kasra) at end to show it's indefinite (like saying 'a mercy' not 'the mercy'). This shows how Salih and the believers were saved - through Allah's mercy.
This is a basic preposition that shows something coming from somewhere or someone
Added نا at the end to show this means 'us'
The ن of مِن combines with the following ن, shown by the shadda mark. This is just a pronunciation change and doesn't affect the meaning.
This is a preposition meaning 'from' that connects to the word خِزْىِ (disgrace/shame) that follows it
The و at the beginning adds 'and', connecting this phrase to what came before. The full phrase وَمِنْ خِزْىِ means 'and from the disgrace/shame'
This root is about shame and disgrace
Changed from action to the thing itself by adding vowel marks. Like how 'humiliate' becomes 'humiliation'.
Added kasra at the end to show this disgrace belongs to 'that day' (يَوْمِئِذٍ). It's like saying 'that day's disgrace'.
This root is about time and days
Connected the root letters together with vowel marks to make the basic noun 'day'
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it
Added ئِذٍ to specify 'that particular day' being referenced in the story

This is a special word that makes what comes after it stronger or more certain. The doubled ن (shown by ّ) makes the emphasis even stronger. Here it emphasizes that 'your Lord' (رَبَّكَ) is truly powerful.
This root is about lordship and ownership
The root letters r-b-b got a shadda (double letter) and fatha marks to become 'rabb', meaning the one who takes care of everything. The fatha at the end shows it's being affected by inna

steps.m: your complete nurturer (acted upon by إِنَّ)
steps.n: Adding ك shows this nurturer belongs to or is connected to you. The word is affected by إِنَّ which came before it in the ayah.
This is a personal pronoun meaning 'he'. In this ayah, it refers back to رَبَّكَ (your Lord) and connects to the following descriptions ٱلْقَوِىُّ ٱلْعَزِيزُ (the All-Strong, the All-Mighty).
This root is about strength and power
Added shadda to intensify the meaning
Added ال to make it definite
Added damma to show this is one of Allah's names describing His attribute of absolute strength
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Forms emphatic adjective on pattern فَعِيل to intensify meaning
ال makes it definite and refers specifically to Allah as this is one of His names, damma shows this is the subject
Added fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word is the main verb of this part of the ayah.

steps.m: and he took/seized (connects to previous ayah)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this action to what happened before
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Added ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple people
Added ال at the beginning and a shadda to make it specific. This word is connected to ظَلَمُوا۟ (they wronged) - it tells us who did the wronging.

steps.m: to be unfair or treat badly

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to show this happened in the past. This word is part of الَّذِينَ (those who) that أَخَذَ (seized) in this ayah.
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action. This describes الَّذِينَ (those who) that were seized.
Changed from basic root letters to a noun meaning 'a single shout' by adding vowels
Added ة to show one specific instance of crying out
Added ال (the) to make it 'the cry' instead of 'a cry'
Added ُ to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence. This word is what 'seized' the wrongdoers in the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create basic past tense verb
Added أَ prefix and changed first vowel to sukun to create Form IV verb meaning 'to become'
Added plural suffix وا with damma before it to indicate 'they' (masculine plural)
Added conjunction prefix فَ meaning 'then/so'
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. It's not derived from a root.
Added ى at the end to complete the preposition. This word connects to دِيٰرِهِمْ (their homes) to show where something is located.

steps.m: to dwell, to settle in a place

steps.n: Changed the root letters for 'dwelling' into a noun meaning 'home' by adding an alif between د and ر
Changed vowels and و to ي to make the plural form
Changed to show possession ('homes of') by dropping the alif and adding kasra at the end
Added هِمْ to show the homes belong to them

steps.m: to lie flat/motionless on the ground

steps.n: Changed to describe someone in this state. This word describes what state they became (أصبحوا) in.
Added plural ending to show multiple people in this state
Added fatha at end to show this describes the state they were in
This is the basic comparison particle that shows similarity

steps.m: it is as if/as though (connects to لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟)
steps.n: Adding أن creates a compound particle that introduces a hypothetical situation. Here it connects to لَّمْ يَغْنَوْا۟ (they had never dwelt) to show a complete change in state.
This is a negative particle that changes the meaning of the verb after it to 'did not' do something. Here it negates يَغْنَوْا۟ (dwell/live), making it mean 'did not dwell'.
Added emphasis by doubling the ل with a shadda mark. This happens because the word comes after كَأَن.
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Added يَ prefix and changed vowels to make it present tense (happening now) instead of past tense (already happened)

steps.m: they dwelt (in relation to لَّمْ meaning 'not')
steps.n: Changed from singular 'he' to plural 'they', and when لَّمْ comes before it, it means 'did not dwell' in the past
Added kasra under ف to show it's a preposition meaning 'in'
Added ها at the end to show this refers to something feminine being discussed
Changed the alif to an extended alif (آ). This is just a writing variation that doesn't change the meaning.
Starting with the basic negative word لَا
Adding أَ at the beginning changes this from a negative word to an attention-getter that warns about what comes next
The alif at the end is lengthened to show emphasis in the warning
Adding shadda changes this from a conditional 'if' to an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger. Here it emphasizes that Thamud definitely disbelieved.

steps.m: Thamud (name of an ancient Arabian tribe)
steps.n: This is a proper noun referring to an ancient Arabian tribe mentioned in the Quran who rejected their prophet Salih

steps.m: Thamud (as the object being referred to)
steps.n: Added ا with fatha to show Thamud is being referred to as the object in this sentence

steps.m: to reject, to cover up, to be ungrateful

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense form. This word relates back to the previous word كَأَن (as if) in the ayah.
The suffix وا changes the subject from 'he' to 'they (masculine)'. Here it refers specifically to the people of Thamud (ثَمُودَ) mentioned in the ayah.
Doubling the ب makes it stronger - shows complete mastery/lordship

steps.m: their Lord (the one who denied by ثَمُودَا۟)
steps.n: Adding هُمْ at the end shows this Lord belongs to them. In this ayah, this refers back to ثَمُودَا۟ (Thamud) who denied their Lord.

steps.m: behold! (introduces the statement إِنَّ ثَمُودَا۟ كَفَرُوا۟ رَبَّهُمْ)
steps.n: This is an attention-drawing word that tells the listener to pay attention to the important statement that follows. In this ayah, it introduces the statement that Thamud rejected their Lord.

steps.m: to be far away from something

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun by adding damma (ُ) and sukun (ْ). Like how 'to be far' becomes 'distance'.

steps.m: away with! (as a curse against ثَمُودَ)
steps.n: Added ًا to turn the word 'distance' into a curse meaning 'away with!', like when you want to push something away. The ًا makes it work like 'shoo!' or 'begone!'

steps.m: Thamud (an ancient Arabian tribe mentioned in the Quran)
steps.n: This is the basic name of the tribe that rejected their prophet Salih
Added لِ at the beginning to show direction or purpose, and َ at the end to show this is the object of the sentence
Added emphasis (shadda) to show strong connection to the word بُعْدًا (away/far) in the ayah, emphasizing the curse upon Thamud
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation
Added لَ at the beginning to add extra emphasis, like saying 'most certainly' instead of just 'certainly'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Added vowels and the middle letter ي changed to ا because it's between two fathas
Added long alif to emphasize the action of coming

steps.m: she came (subject of رُسُلُنَآ - our messengers)
steps.n: Added ت with sukun at the end to show the subject is feminine. This word refers to رُسُلُنَآ (our messengers) in this ayah.

steps.m: to send or be sent as a messenger

steps.n: Added و between second and third letters to show someone who does the action
Changes from 'rasool' to 'rusul' to make it plural - like changing 'book' to 'books'
Added damma at end to show this is the doer of the verb جَآءَتْ (came)
Added نَآ at the end to show these are our messengers. This word is doing the action of coming (جَآءَتْ) in this ayah.
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew, referring to the prophet Abraham. It has no Arabic root.
The fatha at the end shows Ibrahim is receiving the action of 'came to' in the phrase 'Our messengers came to Ibrahim'
Changes the word pattern to create a feminine noun meaning the concept/result of giving good news
Adds the definite article ال to specify this particular good news

steps.m: with the good news (brought to إِبْرٰهِيمَ)
steps.n: Adds the preposition بِ meaning 'with' to show how the good news was brought to Ibrahim

steps.m: with the good news (brought to إِبْرٰهِيمَ)
steps.n: Adds an alif to extend the final vowel sound (this is just a pronunciation helper)
Added fatha marks to make the past tense form. The middle letter و changes to ا because it comes between two fathas.

steps.m: they said (referring to the messengers/رُسُلُنَآ who came to Ibrahim)
steps.n: Adding وا at the end makes it plural masculine, changing from 'he' to 'they'. This refers back to the messengers (رُسُلُنَآ) mentioned earlier in the verse.

steps.m: to be at peace, safe, secure

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun meaning the state of peace
The alif is written as a long vowel mark (ٰ) above the letter instead of as a full letter

steps.m: peace! (as a greeting to the angels who visited Ibrahim)
steps.n: Adding tanween fatha (ًا) makes this word function as a greeting. This is how Ibrahim greeted his visitors who were angels.
Added fathas to make it past tense. This is the basic form before the weak letter change.
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter between two fathas. This word is followed by سَلٰمٌ (peace) which is what was said.
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters into a basic noun form
Adding alif after second letter makes it into a noun meaning a state of peace
Adding tanween dammah makes it an indefinite noun meaning 'a peace'

Added fatha to show proper pronunciation. مَا is a versatile word that can mean 'not' or 'what' depending on context. Here it means 'not' because it's negating لَبِثَ (delayed/waited) in the next part of the ayah.
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous action. فَ means things happen in sequence, like 'and then' or 'so then' in English.
Adding short vowels (fatha-kasra-fatha) to the root letters to make it mean 'he stayed'
This is a connecting word (particle) that joins two parts of the sentence. Here it connects between لَبِثَ (he delayed/waited) and جَآءَ (he came), showing what happened after the waiting.
Added vowel marks (fatha) to the root letters to make it mean 'he came' in the past tense
Changed to hollow verb pattern by adding alif after first letter
The long alif (آ) adds emphasis to show the action was definite or significant
Changed the root meaning of quick movement into a noun for a baby cow (calf), which is known for moving quickly and playfully
Added بِ at the beginning to mean 'with' and ٍ at the end to mean 'a'. This word is connected to the verb جَآءَ (he came) earlier in the verse, showing that Ibrahim brought this calf.
Forms an adjective describing meat that has been thoroughly roasted until tender. In this ayah, it's describing عِجْل (calf/young cow).
The tanwin kasra (ٍ) shows this word is describing the عِجْل (calf) mentioned earlier in the ayah, indicating it was brought as a well-roasted meal for the guests.
This is a basic time particle that means 'when' something happens
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens after something else, connecting it to the previous events

steps.m: to see with eyes or mind

steps.n: Added vowels to make past tense verb meaning 'he saw'
The final letter changes to alif maqsurah due to being a weak verb
The alif is written with a special long form (آ) to show it's an extended sound

steps.m: to extend or reach out with the hand

steps.n: Root letters ي-د-ي became the noun 'hand' (يَد)
Changed to broken plural form by adding hamza at start and sukun in middle
Added ي at the end to make it feminine plural, showing multiple hands

steps.m: their hands (referring to the guests' hands that did not reach the food)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show possession by them, with fatha before it to connect the sounds
This is a negative particle that negates the verb تَصِلُ (reach) that comes after it, changing its meaning from 'reach' to 'do not reach'
Added fatha mark on ل to show correct pronunciation
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changes from past to present tense by adding ي at the start. The middle vowel changes from a (fatha) to i (kasra)

steps.m: it reaches (referring to أَيْدِيَهُمْ - their hands)
steps.n: Changed يَ to تَ to show the subject is 'it' (referring to their hands), since hands is treated as feminine in Arabic
This is the basic preposition meaning 'to' or 'towards'
The ى changes to ي before adding pronouns. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.

steps.m: to him (reaching him in the ayah)
steps.n: Added هِ (him) to show who is being reached. In this ayah, it refers to their hands not reaching him (Prophet Ibrahim).

steps.m: to not recognize or find strange

steps.n: Added vowels to make past tense verb. This word describes Ibrahim's reaction to his guests in the story, connecting to the next verb أَوْجَسَ (he felt).
Added هُمْ to show who was found strange - referring to the visiting angels who appeared as guests

steps.m: to sense something secretly or internally
steps.n: This is the basic root meaning of internal perception or feeling
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Adding hamza alif at the beginning and sukun on the middle letter changes the verb to Form IV, which intensifies the meaning
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this action with what came before it in the verse
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation

steps.m: from them (referring to the angels who were guests)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show this refers to multiple people (the visiting angels). In this ayah, it shows Ibrahim feeling fear from these guests when they didn't eat his food.
This is the basic root meaning of fear
Root meaning 'to fear' becomes the noun 'fear' by adding the 'i' sound (kasra) after first letter
Added ة to show one specific instance of fear
Added -an ending to show this is a specific feeling of fear (like saying 'he felt a fear')
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas
Added وا at the end to show a group of people (they) did the action
This is a negative particle used before a verb to tell someone not to do something. Here it's used with تَخَفْ (fear) to say 'don't fear'
This is the basic root meaning of fear
Added vowels to connect the letters. The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter.
Added تَ at beginning to mean 'you' and changed vowels to make it present tense
The ا is removed and a sukun (ْ) is added to make it a command. This comes after لا which makes it mean 'don't fear!'
This is a special word that adds emphasis to statements, like adding 'indeed' or 'truly' in English
Added نا at the end to mean 'we'. This shows who is being emphasized.
The ا is written as آ when it comes at the end of certain words. This is just a spelling change and doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he sent' to 'was sent' by adding أُ at start and changing inside vowels to make it passive - like changing from doer to receiver of action
Added نَآ to show 'we' as the subject. The long alif at the end shows emphasis because this word follows إِنَّ (indeed/verily) earlier in the sentence.
This is the basic form of the preposition showing direction or destination

Added the small alif (ٰ) which is a pronunciation helper showing that the ى is pronounced as a long 'aa' sound. This connects to the word قَوْمِ (people) in the ayah, showing who/what is being directed towards.
The root relates to standing, but as a noun it means 'people/folk'
Changed from action to noun by adding fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter. The meaning shifts from the action of standing to refer to a group of people who stand together as a community.
Added kasra at the end to show this connects to the word after it (Lut). This is like saying 'people of Lut' in English.
This is the proper name of the prophet Lot (لوط). Since it's a proper name, we start with the basic form rather than a root.
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is the possessed thing in the phrase 'people of Lot'. This connects it to the word قَوْمِ (people) that comes before it.
Adding ة makes this word specifically mean a female/woman
Adds conjunction 'and' at the beginning
Adds nominative case ending to show this is the subject of قَآئِمَةٌ (standing)
Adds 'his' (هُ) at the end with a small elongation mark
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changed from past tense verb (stood) to active participle (standing/one who stands) by adding alif after first letter and kasra before last letter

steps.m: standing (describing a feminine person/thing that is standing)
steps.n: Added ة to show this describes a feminine person or thing (in this case, describing امرأة meaning 'wife' from earlier in the verse)

steps.m: standing (describing his wife who was standing)
steps.n: Added ٌ (tanween) to show this is describing something new being mentioned. The آ is just a writing style for ا.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense form

steps.m: she laughed/smiled (Sarah, the wife of Ibrahim)
steps.n: Added feminine past tense marker ت with sukun to show the subject is female. This refers to Sarah (Ibrahim's wife) in the story.

steps.m: so she laughed/smiled (after hearing the news)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happened as a result of the previous events - her laughing came after hearing the news from the angels
Connected the root letters with fatha to create basic past tense verb
Added shadda to middle letter to show emphasis and intensification of giving good news
Added نا to show 'we' did the action. The middle letter gets sukun (ْ) before نا

steps.m: we gave her good news (acted on)
steps.n: Added ها (meaning 'her') to show who received the good news

steps.m: and we gave her good news
steps.n: Added فَ (with fatha) at beginning to mean 'and then' or 'so', showing this happened as a result of what came before
This is a proper name that refers to Prophet Ibrahim's son. It has no Arabic root since it comes from Hebrew.

steps.m: with Isaac (object of فَبَشَّرْنٰهَا - We gave her glad tidings)
steps.n: Added بِ at the start to show this is what she was given good news about. This connects to the word فَبَشَّرْنٰهَا (We gave her glad tidings) in the ayah.
This is a preposition showing where something comes from. Here it introduces where يَعْقُوبَ (Jacob) comes from.
Added وَ at the start to connect this phrase to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English

steps.m: to be behind or hidden from view
steps.n: Added basic vowel marks to connect the letters
Changes from a verb (to be hidden) to a noun (the place/direction that is hidden)
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to إِسْحٰقَ (Isaac) in the phrase 'behind Isaac'
This is a proper name that comes from Hebrew. It has no Arabic root since it's a foreign name.

steps.m: Isaac (object of بَشَّرْنٰهَا/we gave her glad tidings)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this is the object of the verb بَشَّرْنٰهَا (we gave her glad tidings). It tells us that Isaac is what she was given glad tidings about.
This is a proper name that comes after the mention of Isaac in this ayah
The fatha at the end shows this name is being mentioned as part of a list after the word 'and' (وَ)
Adding vowel marks to make basic past tense verb pattern

steps.m: she said (speaking to her husband about having a child)
steps.n: The root قَوَلَ changes to قَالَ (said) first, then adding تْ at the end makes it 'she said'. The تْ shows a female did the action.
This is the basic concept representing distress or misfortune
Added vowel marks to connect the letters into a noun
Added ة (written as ت here) to make it feminine, which makes it more specific and personal
Added ى at the end to mean 'my' - like saying 'Oh my distress!'
Added يٰ at the beginning to make it an exclamation of distress
Added short vowels (fat-ha marks) to make it past tense 'he begot/fathered'
Changed from past tense 'he' to present tense 'I' by adding أ at start and changing vowels
Added hamza at beginning to make it a question expressing surprise
This is a personal pronoun meaning 'I'. In this ayah, Sarah is expressing amazement at the idea of having a child at her old age.
Added و (and) at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before. The small mark (۠) at the end shows this is being spoken with emphasis.

steps.m: to be unable, to lack strength

steps.n: The word changed from a root meaning 'weak/unable' to mean 'old woman' by adding و which makes it describe someone with that quality

steps.m: old woman (subject of أَنَا۠ - I am)
steps.n: Added tanween damma to show this is the predicate (description) after أَنَا۠ (I am) in the ayah
This is a demonstrative word used to point to something or someone being discussed. In this ayah, Sarah is referring to her husband.
The و at the beginning connects this phrase to her previous statement about herself being old. She's saying 'I am old AND this husband of mine is old'
Connected the root letters together and added vowel marks to make the basic noun form

steps.m: my husband (speaking about her husband in this ayah)
steps.n: Added ي with kasra at the end to show possession. In this ayah, Sarah is speaking about her husband Ibrahim (عليه السلام).
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to create the noun form meaning 'old man'
Added ًا at the end to show this describes the state of 'my husband' in the sentence. Like saying 'my husband, being an old man'
This is an emphasis particle that makes what comes after it stronger. In this ayah, it emphasizes that 'this is indeed an amazing thing!'
Basic demonstrative word used to point to something nearby
Added alif above ه to show this points to something nearby
Added ا at the end to show this is masculine singular. In this ayah, it's pointing to the word بَعْلِى (my husband).
The root relates to wanting or being a thing
Forms the basic noun meaning 'thing' by connecting the letters with vowel marks. This word will be the subject after إِنَّ (indeed) in this ayah.
Adds tanween damma to show this is any general thing, not a specific one

steps.m: indeed (it is) a thing (subject after إِنَّ)
steps.n: Adds لَ at the beginning to emphasize that this is definitely true. This emphasis works together with إِنَّ (indeed) that comes before it in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Added ي between second and third letters and changed middle vowel to kasra to make it into an adjective showing a strong quality
Added tanween damma to show this is a general description, not referring to anything specific
Added vowels (a-a-a) to make it 'he said' in the past tense
Changed middle letter from وَ to ا and added وا at the end to make it 'they said' instead of 'he said'
Added optional marks (small و above alif and small circle) that don't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to form the basic past tense verb
Added ت prefix for 'you' and changed vowels to show present tense
Added ين to show it's talking to a female

steps.m: are you (feminine) amazed? (at أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ - the command of Allah)
steps.n: Added أَ at beginning to make it a question. This word is connected to 'أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ' (the command of Allah) which explains what the person is amazed about.
This is a basic preposition particle that shows something comes from somewhere or someone

steps.m: from (connects أَمْرِ with what comes before)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word. This word connects 'command/matter' (أَمْرِ) with what comes before it.
Adding sukun on middle letter creates the noun form meaning 'matter' or 'affair'
Changed from 'commanded' to 'command/matter' and got kasra ending because it belongs to Allah (like saying 'Allah's command')
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns

This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes the ل, and the kasra at the end shows it belongs to or connects with something else in the sentence.
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and form the basic noun
Added ة to make it a feminine noun

steps.m: mercy (subject of this part of the ayah)
steps.n: Added ُ to show this is the subject. The ة changes to ت when adding endings. This word is connected to اللَّهِ (Allah's) in the ayah to mean 'the mercy of Allah'.
Added ال to make it specific and changed the first letter, making it a unique name for The One God
Added shadda on ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added kasra at end to show possession. This word is possessed by أَمْرِ (command) in the phrase 'command of Allah'
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels
Added -aat ending to make it mean 'many blessings' instead of 'he blessed'
Adding damma to show this is a subject along with رَحْمَتُ (mercy)
Adding هُ to show these are Allah's blessings
Adding conjunction و (and) at beginning
This is a common preposition in Arabic meaning 'upon' or 'on'. Since it's a particle, we start with its basic form rather than a root.
The ى changes to يْ before adding pronouns. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect meaning.
The suffix كُمْ shows this is addressing multiple people (you all)

steps.m: to dwell, to be familiar with

steps.n: Basic noun form meaning 'family' - from root meaning 'to dwell together'
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by Allah's mercy (رَحْمَتُ) in this ayah
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make them pronounceable. The ي gets a sukun (ْ) because it's a weak letter in the middle.
Adding ال makes it specific - not just any house

steps.m: of the house (possessed by أَهْلَ - people/family of)
steps.n: The kasra at the end shows this word belongs to or is connected to something else. Here it's connected to أَهْلَ forming the phrase 'people of the house'.
This is an emphasis word that makes statements stronger, like adding 'indeed' or 'certainly' in English
Added هُ at the end to show who or what is being talked about (in this case, referring back to Allah's command mentioned earlier in the verse)
Added the small و mark (ۥ) which is just a writing helper to show the هُ more clearly - it doesn't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The ي between the second and third root letters with kasra before it makes this word mean 'full of praise' or 'worthy of praise'
The tanween dammah (ٌ) shows this is one of Allah's qualities mentioned in this ayah along with مَّجِيدٌ (glorious)
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this describes someone who has lots of glory/praise

steps.m: one who is full of glory/praise
steps.n: Added ٌ at the end to show this is describing someone or something in general

steps.m: most glorious, intensely praiseworthy (describing اللَّهِ - Allah)
steps.n: Added shadda on م to show this is the highest possible level of glory and praise. In this ayah, it's describing Allah.
This is the base word meaning 'when' - it introduces a time condition, showing that one thing happened after another
Added فَ at the beginning to show this happens right after something else, connecting it to the previous events
Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word shows what went away from Ibrahim, as mentioned in the ayah.
This is a basic preposition that shows movement or distance away from something
Added sukun (ْ) on the ن to show where the word stops. This helps with pronunciation but doesn't change the meaning.
This is a proper name that was borrowed into Arabic. It doesn't have an Arabic root.
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object (receiver) of the action in ذَهَبَ عَنْ (went away from)

steps.m: to be frightened, to feel fear

steps.n: Changed from root letters to a noun meaning 'fear' by adding vowels
Adding ال makes it refer to the specific fear that Ibrahim was feeling

steps.m: the fear, the terror (subject of ذَهَبَ)
steps.n: Adding damma ending because this fear is the subject (doer) of went away (ذَهَبَ)
The word means 'fear' and is doing the action (subject)
The middle letter ي changes to ا with a special elongation mark (آ) because it's a hollow verb. Added past tense voweling.
Adding ت makes the verb feminine
Added هُ (meaning 'him') to the end to show who she came to

steps.m: and she came to him (referring to إِبْرٰهِيمَ)
steps.n: Added conjunction prefix و meaning 'and'. The 'him' refers to Ibrahim mentioned earlier in the ayah.
Changed from a verb root meaning 'to give good news' into a simple noun meaning 'good news' by adding the vowel marks ُ (u) and ْ (sukoon)
Added ى at the end to make it mean 'glad tidings' - a special happy announcement
Added 'the' (ال) at the start to make it specific good news
Added ى at the end to make it a feminine noun

steps.m: to argue or debate back and forth

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter to show the action is done with extra effort or repeatedly
Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
Added نَا at the end to show who he is arguing with (us)
This is a basic preposition that shows location or being inside something. In this ayah, it connects the verb يُجٰدِلُنَا (arguing with Us) to قَوْمِ لُوطٍ (the people of Lut).
Added the letter ى at the end to complete the word. This is just how this preposition is written in Arabic - the ى doesn't change the meaning.
Added fatha on first letter and sukun (represented by و) on middle letter to create a noun meaning 'people' - those who stand/gather together
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what follows it (لُوطٍ). This creates the meaning 'people of Lut'
This is the basic form of the proper name Lut/Lot, a prophet mentioned in the Quran
Added the tanwin kasra (ٍ) at the end to show this word follows قَوْمِ (people of) in the phrase 'people of Lot'
This is the basic form of the word that adds emphasis to what follows
Adding the vowels and shadda makes the emphasis stronger. This word emphasizes that Ibrahim really is gentle and humble.
This is a proper name that comes from ancient Hebrew. It doesn't have an Arabic root.
Added vowel marks to show this is the subject being described as 'forbearing' (حَلِيمٌ) in this verse. The َ (fatha) at the end shows it's being acted upon by the word إِنَّ that comes before it.
Added vowels and ي to show this describes someone who has these qualities strongly

steps.m: truly very gentle and patient (describing إِبْرٰهِيمَ)
steps.n: Added لَ at the beginning to emphasize that Ibrahim definitely has these qualities

steps.m: one who frequently turns (to Allah) (describing إِبْرٰهِيمَ)
steps.n: The tanween shows this is an indefinite adjective describing Ibrahim in this ayah

steps.m: to return or turn back to (especially to Allah in repentance)

steps.n: Changed from root meaning 'return' to a word describing someone who is returning, by adding vowels and changing و to ي
Added مُ at the start to show someone who does this action repeatedly

steps.m: one who constantly returns (to Allah) (describing إِبْرٰهِيمَ)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is describing Ibrahim. This word appears in a series of descriptions: 'Indeed, Ibrahim was forbearing, imploring, constantly returning (to Allah)'
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew that appears frequently in the Quran
Added يا at the beginning to show someone is calling out to Abraham. The long alif (ٰ) and hamza (ٓ) are added to emphasize the call.
Added ُ (damma) at the end to show this is the main person being spoken to
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: away from (connects to أَعْرِضْ - turn away)
steps.n: This is a primary preposition that shows something moving away from something else. In this ayah, it connects to the command أَعْرِضْ (turn away), showing what to turn away from.
Basic demonstrative word that points to something nearby
Added ه at the beginning to draw attention to what's being pointed at
Added alif above ه (ٰ) to show elongation in pronunciation
Added fatha (َ) to show the 'a' sound after ذ
Changed final alif to آ to show extra elongation in pronunciation
This is a special emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger - like adding an exclamation mark in English. It's one of the basic building blocks of Arabic that doesn't come from a root.

steps.m: indeed he/it (referring to the command/matter of your Lord)
steps.n: Added هُ (meaning 'he/it') at the end to show we're talking about something specific - in this case, the command or matter mentioned in أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ (the command of your Lord) that comes later in the ayah.
The small mark ۥ is just a pronunciation helper that shows this is a pronoun suffix. It doesn't change the meaning or grammatical function.
This is the basic form of the word - a particle that adds emphasis
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation. When قَدْ comes before a past tense verb like جَآءَ (came), it emphasizes that the action has definitely happened.
Added short 'a' sounds (fatha marks) to the root letters to make it mean 'he came' in the past
Changed to a hollow verb pattern by adding alif after first letter
Connected letters and added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create noun meaning 'command/matter'
Added damma at end to show this is the subject (doer) of the verb جَآءَ (has come) in this ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha to create the basic word form

steps.m: master/lord (one who nurtures and takes care of others)
steps.n: The shadda combines the repeated ب and makes the meaning stronger, showing complete mastery and care
Adding kasra (the i-sound) at the end shows possession - like saying 'my Lord' or 'Lord of'
Adding كَ shows this is your lord. The whole phrase أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ means 'command of your Lord'
Starting with the emphatic particle إنّ which adds certainty to statements

steps.m: indeed they (referring to the people of Ibrahim)
steps.n: Added هم to show who is being talked about - in this case, the people Ibrahim was warned about

steps.m: and indeed they (connecting to the previous statement about Allah's command)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this statement with the previous one about Allah's command coming

steps.m: coming (it is coming to عَذَابٌ - punishment)
steps.n: The root 'to come' becomes 'coming' by adding a long alif at the start and a kasra (i sound) in the middle. It tells us that punishment is on its way.

steps.m: coming to them (referring to the punishment coming to the people being addressed)
steps.n: Added هِمْ at the end to show who the punishment is coming to. This refers back to the people being warned in the ayah.
Added vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past
Changed the verb into a noun by adding alif after the second root letter
Added double dammah to make it indefinite (like saying 'a punishment' instead of 'the punishment')
Root meaning 'different' becomes a noun meaning 'other than'
Added the ُ (damma) at the end to show this word describes عَذَابٌ (punishment) in the ayah

steps.m: to send back or return something

steps.n: Added vowel marks and doubled the middle letter د to make the basic past tense verb meaning 'he sent back'
Added مَ at the start and و after د to change it from 'he sent back' to 'something that was sent back' (like changing 'return' to 'returned thing')

steps.m: something that was sent back (describing عَذَابٌ/punishment)
steps.n: Added tanween kasra to show this describes the punishment (عَذَابٌ) that cannot be sent back
This is a time particle that shows one event happened right before another. Here it connects to when the messengers came to Lut.
Added و at the beginning to connect this event with what happened before it in the story.
Added fatha vowels to connect the letters and make it past tense
Changed to common past tense pattern for hollow verbs (verbs with middle weak letter)

Adding ت with sukun (تْ) at the end shows the subject is feminine (our messengers). The messengers are referred to as feminine here even though they were male, because plural non-human nouns are treated as feminine singular in Arabic.
The pattern رَسُول shows someone whose job is to deliver messages (like a mail carrier)
Changes from singular to plural by changing the middle vowel and dropping the long vowel, making it shorter
Adds نَا at the end to show these are 'our' messengers. This word is doing the action of 'coming' in the verse.
This is the proper name of Prophet Lot (peace be upon him)

steps.m: Lot (as the object of سِىٓءَ - was troubled)
steps.n: Added tanween alif (ًا) to show this is the object being affected by the verb سِىٓءَ (was troubled)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: he was troubled/made to feel bad
steps.n: The verb changed to show something happened to someone rather than them doing it
Added kasra (ِ) to help with pronunciation when connecting to next part
Added ه with kasra to show this applies to 'him/it'

steps.m: by/with them (referring to رُسُلُنَا - our messengers)
steps.n: Added م with sukun to change 'him' to 'them', referring back to the messengers (رُسُلُنَا) mentioned earlier in the ayah

steps.m: to be/become tight, narrow, or distressed

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters and show past tense
Changed to hollow verb pattern with alif

steps.m: and he became distressed (about بِهِمْ - them)
steps.n: Added و with fatha to connect this word to what came before it. In this ayah, the distress is about 'them' (بِهِمْ) - referring to the visitors.
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'by' or 'with'
Added ه with kasra to show this refers to 'him' or 'it'
Added م with sukun to change 'him' to 'them', referring back to the messengers (رُسُلُنَا) mentioned earlier in the verse

steps.m: capacity (like how much one can handle)
steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make a noun meaning capacity or ability to handle something

steps.m: capacity/ability (object of ضَاقَ - became distressed with)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb ضَاقَ (became distressed). In this context, it means Lut became distressed in his capacity to handle the situation.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show past tense meaning
The middle weak letter و changes to ا due to standard weak letter rules
Added و at the beginning to connect this action with what came before it in the verse
This is the basic attention-drawing part that starts many demonstrative pronouns
The alif is written as a small mark above the ه to show it's shortened in pronunciation
The ذَ makes it specifically point to something nearby
The final alif helps with pronunciation and shows it's being used as a subject (this is a difficult day)
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to create the basic noun form. The و stays as is because it's essential to the word's meaning.
Added tanween (double damma) to show this is indefinite (like saying 'a day' instead of 'the day'). This word is being described by عَصِيبٌ (difficult/distressing) in this ayah.
The root conveys the idea of binding or pressing together
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed the middle vowel to kasra and added ي to show intensity of the quality
Added two dots on top (tanween) to show this is describing something in a general way
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
Changed to hollow verb pattern with alif in middle
Added و at the beginning to mean 'and'
Added ه with a small و (ۥ) after it to show it's pronounced 'hu' not 'hi', meaning 'him'

steps.m: to stand or rise up (basic meaning)

steps.n: The vowels transform the root into a noun meaning a group that stands together, which came to mean 'people' or 'nation'
The ُ shows this word is doing the action in the sentence - they are the ones who came

steps.m: his people (as the doers of جَاءَ/came)
steps.n: Adds هُۥ to show these are his people specifically. The small و mark (ۥ) helps with pronunciation.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Added يُ at start and changed vowels to show present passive voice - something is being done to the subject
Added ون to show multiple people are being rushed
Present tense verb meaning 'they rush/hurry' - the ون ending shows it's multiple people doing the action
This is a preposition that shows movement or direction towards something
Changed the final ى to ي with sukun to prepare for adding the pronoun. This is just a spelling change to help connect the next part.

steps.m: to him (referring back to the person being rushed to)
steps.n: Added هِ at the end to show this means 'to him'. The ـهِ shows it's happening to him or directed at him.
Basic preposition meaning 'from'. This word doesn't come from a root - it's a basic building block of Arabic.
Adding وَ at the beginning connects this word to what came before it, like saying 'and' in English

steps.m: to be in front of or before something

steps.n: Root meaning 'before/in front' becomes a simple noun meaning 'before' (like 'before dinner')
Adds damma to show this word is in nominative case
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the letters
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas in past tense
Added وا to show multiple people (they) did the action in the past. This refers back to 'his people' (قَوْمُهُۥ) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the past tense verb form
Adding يَ at start and changing vowels (َ to ْ on first root letter, َ to ُ on last root letter) creates present tense

steps.m: they work/act/do (referring to the people who were doing evil deeds)
steps.n: Adding ونَ makes it plural, showing multiple people doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to the people who were committing evil acts.
Root س-و-أ becomes سَيِّئ by changing و to ي with shadda, making it mean 'very evil/harmful'
Added ة to specify one instance of evil/harmful action
Changed ة to ات to show multiple evil deeds
Added ٱل to specify these particular evil deeds. This word is what is being done in the phrase 'they used to do evil deeds'
Added kasra (i) sound at end to show this word is being possessed by or connected to something else
Added fathas to make past tense form. This is the theoretical form before the weak letter change.

steps.m: he said (introducing what he tells his people)
steps.n: The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change in Arabic for weak letters.

steps.m: to stand together as a group

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the letters. The root meaning of standing together became associated with a group of people who stand as one.
Added يٰ at start to call out to someone (like saying 'Hey!') and ِ at end to show it means 'my people'
Starting with the basic demonstrative element ها, which changes to هؤ when building the plural form
Added لاء to make it refer to multiple things/people that are being pointed to
Added vowel marks and elongation signs to show proper pronunciation and grammatical position

steps.m: to build, establish, or create offspring

steps.n: Changes root meaning 'build/create' into 'daughter' by replacing final ي with feminine ت (ta)
Changes singular 'daughter' to plural 'daughters' by adding ات
Adds first person possessive 'my' by adding ى at the end
Basic demonstrative pronoun base with damma
Added نَّ with shadda to make it feminine plural. This pronoun refers back to Prophet Lut's daughters (بَنَاتِى) mentioned in the previous part of the verse.
Basic root meaning related to purity and cleanliness
Added vowels to make it past tense - 'became pure'
Added أ at beginning and changed vowels to make comparative form, meaning 'more/most pure'. This word describes 'my daughters' (بَنَاتِى) in this verse.
Basic preposition meaning 'for' or 'to'. In this ayah, it shows who the daughters are being offered to.

steps.m: for you all (referring to قَوْمِ/my people)
steps.n: Adding كُمْ means 'you all' (addressing multiple people). Here Prophet Lut is addressing his people (قَوْمِ) who came rushing to him.
