steps.m: to watch over carefully, guard, observe closely
steps.n: This root is about keeping an eye on something or someone
Added ي between second and third root letters to show someone who does this action as a strong quality or regular habit

steps.m: a watchful observer (describes إِنِّى مَعَكُمْ - indeed I am with you)
steps.n: The tanween (ٌ) shows this is indefinite, like saying 'a' or 'an' in English. This word completes the meaning of 'Indeed I am with you as a watchful observer.'
Added و at the beginning to connect this clause to what came before it
Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to the root letters to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he came'
Changed to hollow verb pattern by adding alif after first letter
Extended the alif to show the length/intensity of coming
Changed from verb 'to command' into the noun 'command' by adding the vowel marks fatḥa and sukūn
Added damma to show this is the subject (doer) of the verb جَآءَ (came) in this ayah
Added نا to show the command belongs to us. This word is still the subject of جَآءَ (came)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The doubled ج shows this is done with extra effort or intensity. The weak letter و changes to ى at the end.
Changed the ending to make it easier to add نا (meaning 'us/our')
Added نا at the end to show 'we' did the action
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the name correctly

steps.m: Shu'ayb (as the object being saved in نَجَّيْنَا)
steps.n: Added ًا at the end to show Shu'ayb is the one being saved in the phrase 'We saved Shu'ayb'
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that refers to someone or something
Adding ال makes it more specific and adds a shadda on the ذ
Adding ين at the end makes it refer to multiple people
Adding و at the beginning connects this word to what came before it. The alif gets a special mark (wasla) showing it connects with the و
Added alif after first letter to strengthen the meaning from just trusting to having complete faith
Added وا at the end to show many people did this action
This is a preposition meaning 'with'. Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation.
Added هُ at the end to mean 'him', referring back to Prophet Shu'ayb who was mentioned earlier in the verse. The ۥ mark is just to show proper pronunciation.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters together
The ة at the end makes this a noun meaning one instance or type of mercy

steps.m: with mercy (connected to نَجَّيْنَا - we saved)
steps.n: The بِ at the start means 'with' and shows how the saving was done. The ٍ at the end shows this is any mercy, not a specific one.
This is a basic preposition meaning 'from'
Added نا at the end to show this means 'us'. The shadda (ّ) appears because the ن from مِن combines with the ن from نا.
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this is past tense
The ت shows this is feminine because الصَّيْحَةُ (the blast/cry) is feminine
The و at the beginning means 'and'
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
The ين ending makes it refer to multiple people
Adding ال makes it mean 'those who'. This word is describing the people who believed with Shu'ayb, as shown by ءَامَنُوا۟ (they believed) that comes after it.
Added fathas to connect the letters and make the past tense form
Added وا at the end to show that a group of people (they) did the action. This word is connected to أَخَذَتِ (seized) in the ayah, showing who was seized.
Connected the root letters together with vowel marks to make it pronounceable

steps.m: a single loud cry or blast
steps.n: The ة at the end makes it refer to one specific instance of the sound
Adding the definite article makes the noun definite
Adding shadda makes the sound more powerful. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb أَخَذَتِ (it seized) in this ayah.
The ُ shows this word is doing the action in the sentence - it is what seized them

steps.m: to enter morning state, to become
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Adds a-a-a vowels to the root letters to make a simple past tense verb meaning 'he entered morning'
Adding أ at start changes meaning to 'becoming' or 'entering into a state'
Adding وا changes 'he' to 'they' (masculine plural)

steps.m: then they became/entered into a state
steps.n: فَ connects this action to what happened before it, showing sequence
This is a preposition showing location or position. In this ayah, it shows that the people were 'in' their homes (دِيٰرِهِمْ).

steps.m: to move around in a circle or stay in a place
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Changed the root 'to move/stay' into a noun meaning 'home' by adding fatha - like how a home is where you stay
Changed the vowel to kasra and added ي to make it plural (more than one home)
Changed يَ to ٰ because it's easier to pronounce
Added هِمْ at the end to show these homes belong to them

steps.m: to lie face down, to fall motionless
steps.n: This is the basic meaning of the root
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Changes from verb (action) to noun (doer of action)

steps.m: fallen motionless ones (describing them in أَصْبَحُوا)
steps.n: Added ين to show this describes many people. This word tells us how they ended up after أَصْبَحُوا (they became).
This is the comparison particle ك that shows similarity
Added fatha to help with pronunciation
Added أن which combines with كَ to create the meaning of 'as if/as though'. This word introduces a hypothetical scenario in this ayah, comparing the state of Madyan to something that never existed.

steps.m: not (makes a present verb into past negative)
steps.n: This is a negative particle that changes the meaning of verbs that come after it. When used with يَغْنَوْا (live/dwell) in this ayah, it makes it mean 'did not live/dwell'.

steps.m: not (makes a present verb into past negative)
steps.n: The ل gets a shadda (double letter mark) because it comes after a word ending in ن, which merges into the ل. The shadda makes the sound stronger but doesn't change the meaning.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the past tense verb
Added يَ at the start to make it present tense. The middle letter got quiet (sukun). The final ي became ى because it's at the end of the word

steps.m: they dwelt (referring to the people of Madyan)
steps.n: Added وْا at the end to show multiple people doing the action. The ى changes to و because it's followed by وا
Added the kasra vowel mark to show the correct pronunciation
Added ها which refers back to their dwellings mentioned in the context
Changed the alif to a long alif (آ) for emphasis in pronunciation
This is the basic attention-getting sound, like saying 'hey!' to get someone to listen
Adding لَا makes the word stronger, turning it from a simple 'hey!' into a stronger call for attention
Changed the action into a noun by adding damma (ُ) on first letter and sukun (ْ) on middle letter
Adding ًا at the end changes it to describe how something happened - in this case, it describes how the people were destroyed or cast away
Added vowels and inserted ي to form the proper noun Madyan, which was an ancient city mentioned in the Quran
Added لِ at beginning to show direction or purpose towards Madyan
The ل got stronger (doubled) because it came after a similar letter
This is a comparison particle that shows similarity
Adding ما makes the comparison more general and connects it to the following verb بَعِدَتْ (were far/distant). Together they mean 'just as' or 'in the same way as'
Added vowels to make a past tense verb showing someone entering into a state of being distant
Added ت to show this is talking about something feminine (like Thamud, the city mentioned after)
This is a proper noun referring to the ancient tribe of Thamud mentioned in the Quran

steps.m: Thamud (as the subject of بَعِدَتْ - became distant)
steps.n: The ُ (damma) at the end shows this is the subject doing the action in the sentence. Here, Thamud is the one that 'became distant'.
This particle shows certainty or completion of an action. Here it emphasizes that the sending of Moses definitely happened.
Adding لَ makes the certainty stronger, like saying 'indeed' or 'truly'
Adding وَ connects this statement to what came before it in the Quran
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start and putting sukun on the first root letter makes the verb causative - showing someone made the sending happen
Adding نَا shows 'we' did the action. This word connects to مُوسَىٰ (Moses) who was the one being sent.
This is a proper name borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian that has been Arabized. It doesn't follow standard Arabic morphological patterns.
Added damma (ُ) on first letter to match standard Arabic pronunciation of this name
Added alif khanjariyah (ٰ) which is a small alif written above the final ى to indicate the long 'aa' sound at the end of the name

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be apparent
a sign or miracle (something that shows or makes clear)
steps.n: Root letters changed to make a noun meaning 'sign'. The middle ي became ا, and ة was added at the end to mean 'one sign'
Changed to plural form by removing ة and adding ات
Added نَا to show these signs belong to Allah (referring to Himself as 'We/Our' for majesty)
Added بِ meaning 'with/by'. This shows these signs were given to Moses as proof of his message.
Added damma and sukun to form basic noun pattern
The ان ending makes it stronger - like going from 'power' to 'complete authority'
Changed alif to alif maqsurah for writing style
Added tanwin kasra to show indefiniteness

steps.m: and an authority (as proof or evidence)
steps.n: Added conjunction prefix meaning 'and'
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Added مُ prefix and changed vowels to show this describes something that makes things clear. This word describes سُلْطٰنٍ (authority) in the ayah.
Added shadda on م for pronunciation after the word وَ
Added tanween kasra to match the case of the word it describes (سُلْطٰنٍ)
This is a basic preposition that shows direction or connection to something. Here it connects to فِرْعَوْنَ (Pharaoh) to show who was being sent to.
Added an alif that looks like ى (called alif maqsurah) at the end. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning.
This is a proper noun borrowed from ancient Egyptian language meaning the ruler of Egypt
Added fatha at the end to show this word is the object of إِلَىٰ (to). In simple terms, something is being directed 'to Pharaoh'.

steps.m: to gather in assembly, to form a council

steps.n: Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it mean 'he gathered' in the past tense
Changed the final vowel to kasra to show it's being possessed
Added conjunction وَ to connect with previous word فِرْعَوْنَ (Pharaoh)
Added possessive pronoun هِ (his) referring to Pharaoh
Added pronunciation helpers: ي with small و above it and ۦ after هِ
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
Added ا at start and doubled the ت to show doing something with more effort and purpose

steps.m: so he followed closely (connected to فِرْعَوْنَ)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this action follows from what was mentioned before about Pharaoh

steps.m: so they followed closely (referring to Pharaoh's people)
steps.n: Added plural marker to show multiple people (Pharaoh's people) did the action
Root letters أ-م-ر became the noun 'command' by adding the vowel marks َ (fatha) and ْ (sukun)
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being followed in the phrase 'they followed the command of Pharaoh'

steps.m: Pharaoh (the title of ancient Egyptian kings)
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from ancient Egyptian language. It doesn't have an Arabic root.
The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon by the word إِلَىٰ (to) that comes before it in the ayah.
This is a common particle in Arabic that can mean different things depending on context. Here it's used for negation.

steps.m: and not (negates the command of فِرْعَوْنَ)
steps.n: Added و to connect this negation with the previous statement about Pharaoh's people following his command

steps.m: and not (negates the command of فِرْعَوْنَ)
steps.n: Added fatha on م and lengthened the final alif for emphasis when pausing on this word
Basic root meaning showing the concept of commanding or ordering
Changed from verb (doing) to noun (thing) by adding vowel marks - like turning 'to command' into 'a command'
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing something in the sentence

steps.m: Pharaoh (the title of ancient Egyptian kings)
steps.n: This is a proper noun borrowed from ancient Egyptian into Arabic
Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word correctly
The fatḥa (َ) at the end shows this word is being acted upon - in this case, Pharaoh is the one being followed

steps.m: to guide, to show the right way
steps.n: Changed from verb to noun meaning
Added ي to make it describe someone who has wisdom

steps.m: with wisdom (describing أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ - Pharaoh's command)
steps.n: Added ب at start to mean 'with'. This word tells us about Pharaoh's command - that it wasn't wise.

steps.m: with wisdom (describing أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ - Pharaoh's command)
steps.n: Added vowel marks to show how to pronounce the word correctly

steps.m: with any wisdom (describing أَمْرُ فِرْعَوْنَ - Pharaoh's command)
steps.n: Added ٍ at the end to show this describes something general (any wisdom). This helps explain that Pharaoh's command had no wisdom at all.
Connected the root letters with fatha to create past tense verb
Changed middle vowel to show being ahead (a quality) instead of leading others (an action)

steps.m: he leads/goes in front (of قَوْمَهُ - his people)
steps.n: Added يَ at beginning to show present/future tense, changed first vowel to sukun, and final vowel to damma to show he is doing the action. This word shows how Pharaoh leads his people to the fire.
Added vowels to make the noun 'people' - the و with sukun (ْ) stays to show it's a group that stands together
Added fatha at the end to show this is the object being led

steps.m: his people (referring to Pharaoh's people whom he leads)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show these are his people specifically
Connected the letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun for 'day'

steps.m: on the day (object of يَقْدُمُ - he leads)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this word is receiving the action of 'leading' in the phrase 'he leads his people on the Day of Resurrection'
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changes verb 'stood' into noun meaning 'the act of standing'
Added ة to make it refer to the specific event of resurrection
Added ٱلْ to specify this is THE resurrection (Day of Judgment)

steps.m: the resurrection (in relation to يَوْمَ - Day of)
steps.n: Added ِ at the end to show connection to يَوْمَ (Day of). The alif is written as ٰ for style.

steps.m: to come to water, to arrive

steps.n: Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'he came to water' in the past
Adding أ at the beginning makes it mean 'cause someone else to do the action'
Added هُمُ to show who was brought (them)

steps.m: so he brought them (to النَّارَ - the Fire)
steps.n: Added فَ at beginning to mean 'so/then'. This word leads into النَّارَ (the Fire) which is what they are being brought to.
The middle letter و changes to ا, and we add a fatha on the first letter to make the noun for 'fire'
Adding ال makes it 'the fire' instead of just 'fire'

steps.m: the fire (object of فَأَوْرَدَهُمُ/so he led them to)
steps.n: Adding fatha at the end shows this is the object being acted upon in the sentence
Changes the root into a word expressing strong disapproval

steps.m: and how evil! (describing الْوِرْدُ the arrival)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement. This word is describing how evil the arrival (الْوِرْدُ) mentioned in the next part will be.

steps.m: to come to water, to arrive at a water source
arrival at water (place or act of arriving)
steps.n: Changed from a verb meaning 'to arrive' into a noun meaning 'arrival' by putting an 'i' sound (kasra) then a silent letter (sukun)

steps.m: the arrival at water (subject of بِئْسَ)
steps.n: Added the definite article ٱلْ and nominative case ending ُ. This word is the subject of بِئْسَ (how evil is) in this ayah.

steps.m: to come to, to arrive at (especially water)

steps.n: Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed from 'he arrived' to 'being arrived at' by adding مَ at start and changing vowels - makes it passive like a place or thing being reached

steps.m: the (place/thing) being arrived at (refers to النَّارَ - the Fire)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ to show this refers to a specific destination (the Fire) that is being arrived at, and added ُ at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a past tense verb
Adding hamza at start makes it causative - now means making someone follow rather than following directly
Changed vowels to make it passive - now shows receiving the action instead of doing it
Adding وا shows multiple people received this action

steps.m: and they were made to follow
steps.n: Adding و at start connects this to previous statement
This is a primitive preposition particle that is not derived from a root. It exists in this basic form to indicate location/containment.
Added kasra vowel mark to show how the word is pronounced
Changed the final ي to ى which is a common spelling variation in the Quran. This word connects to هٰذِهِۦ (this) to show what is being referred to.
The basic pointing word starts with ها which shows something close by
Added ذا to make it more specific for pointing to something
Changed the ending to هِ with kasra to make it feminine
Added a small ي (called yaa saghira) after the word as a pronunciation helper
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to form basic noun
Converting verb root to noun using feminine pattern فَعْلَة which indicates a single instance or unit

steps.m: a curse (object of أُتْبِعُوا - they were followed by)
steps.n: This word is what they were followed by (object of أُتْبِعُوا) in this ayah
Connected the root letters together with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to form the basic noun
Adding fatha at the end shows this word is receiving the action
Adding و at the beginning connects this to the previous statement. This word is connected to ٱلْقِيٰمَةِ (the resurrection) which comes after it.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a basic past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun meaning the action itself
Added ة to make it refer to one specific instance of rising
Added ال to refer specifically to the Day of Resurrection
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being acted upon by something before it in the sentence
Connected the root letters that mean something being bad or harmful
Adding vowels changes it to a past tense verb meaning 'how bad it was'
Changed the verb into a noun by adding fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter
Adding 'al' makes it specific/definite, like changing from 'a support' to 'the support'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Changed to show receiving the action instead of doing it. The م at the start and و in the middle show this is something that was supported by someone else.

steps.m: the supported one (the evil aid mentioned in بِئْسَ ٱلرِّفْدُ)
steps.n: Added ال to specify this particular support, and added ُ at the end to show this is the subject of بِئْسَ (what an evil)
Starting with the basic word ذا which points to something nearby. The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) in this word.
Adding لِ changes it from pointing to something close to pointing to something farther away

steps.m: that (pointing to something far) (refers to the stories that will be told)
steps.n: Adding كَ completes the word and makes it point to something specific that's far away. In this ayah, it points forward to the stories that will be told about the towns.
This is a basic preposition that shows where something starts or comes from. In this ayah, it connects 'news' (أَنۢبَآءِ) with 'the towns' (ٱلْقُرَىٰ), showing the news comes from these towns.

steps.m: to tell news or give information

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to form the basic noun
Changed from single news item to plural by adding أ at start and ا after ب - this is a broken plural form, like changing 'story' to 'stories'
Added kasra at end to show this word follows مِنْ (from) in the ayah

steps.m: to settle, gather in a place

steps.n: Root letters connected with a fat-ha (a) and sukoon (stop mark) to make a noun meaning 'settling'

steps.m: a village/town (a place where people settle)
steps.n: Added ة to make it a noun for a specific place
Changed vowel pattern and removed ة to make plural form
Added the definite article ال to specify these particular villages/towns
Added alif maqsurah (ى) at the end to complete the noun form

steps.m: to tell a story or follow news step by step

steps.n: Changed from basic root letters to 'I tell' by adding u-vowels (dummas) and making middle letter stronger
Added نَ at the beginning to show 'we' are doing the action
Added هُۥ at the end to show what is being told (it)
Starting with the basic preposition that shows something is on top of or affecting something else
The ى changes to يْ before adding a suffix, this is a common spelling change

steps.m: upon you (object of نَقُصُّ - we narrate)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show this affects 'you'. This word is receiving the action of نَقُصُّ (we narrate) in this ayah.
This is a preposition showing something coming from somewhere
Added ها at the end to show this refers to something feminine (in this case, referring back to القُرَىٰ - the towns)
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed the middle و to ا and added ئِ to show someone or something doing the action
Changed regular alif to long alif (آ) - this is just a spelling variation with no meaning change
Added tanween dammah to show this is describing something in general terms
Added vowels and ي to show this describes something that was completely harvested or cut down
Added و to connect this word to قَآئِمٌ (standing) in the ayah, showing two states of the towns mentioned

steps.m: and (some were) mowed down (referring to towns)
steps.n: Added tanween dammah to show this describes some of the towns mentioned in the story
Basic negative/interrogative particle that can mean either 'not' or 'what' depending on context
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before. Here it negates 'We wronged them' (ظَلَمْنٰهُمْ) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed the subject to 'we' by adding نٰ. In this context, this is Allah using the royal 'We' to refer to Himself.
Added هُمْ to show who was wronged - 'them' refers to the disbelieving people mentioned in the verse
Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect this with the previous statement. This creates a contrast between 'We did not wrong them' and 'but they wronged themselves'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added وا at the end to show multiple people did the action. This word is connected to أَغْنَتْ (benefited) in the ayah, showing who didn't receive benefit.
Root letters ن-ف-س became the noun نَفْس (nafs) meaning 'soul' by adding vowel marks
Changed the singular 'self' into plural 'selves' by adding أَ at the beginning and changing vowels
Added fatha to show this word is receiving the action of ظَلَمُوا (they wronged)
Adds 3rd person masculine plural possessive pronoun
Added fatha to show this is the question word 'what' being used as a negation. In this ayah, it negates the verb أَغْنَتْ (benefited).
Added alif with madda (آ) to show long vowel sound. This is just a spelling variation and doesn't change the meaning.
Added فَ (and then) at the beginning to show this follows from the previous statement. This connects to the previous ayah which mentions that they wronged themselves.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start makes it mean 'to make something not need something else' instead of just 'being free from need'

steps.m: she/it availed (in relation to ءَالِهَتُهُمُ - their gods)
steps.n: Adding ت shows that the subject is feminine (their gods). In this ayah, it's saying their gods didn't help them at all.
This is a preposition that shows something moving away from or being separate from something else

steps.m: from/away from them (referring to their gods that they called upon besides Allah)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being moved away from. In this ayah, it refers to the false gods that couldn't help the people when Allah's command came.

steps.m: to worship or be devoted to

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to worship' into noun meaning 'one who is worshipped'
Changes the word to a broken plural form, which is common for non-human plurals in Arabic
Adds 'their' to show possession/ownership
Adds a final vowel to show this word is the subject of 'they call upon' (يَدْعُونَ) in this verse
Base feminine relative marker with kasra and alif maqsura
Added ال with shadda to create the complete relative pronoun. This word refers to their false gods mentioned in the previous part of the verse.

steps.m: to call upon, invite, or pray to

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters and make the past tense. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.
Added يَ at the start to show present tense, changed middle vowel to sukun, and last vowel to damma. The ا changes back to و.

steps.m: they call/invite (referring to their false gods in this ayah)
steps.n: Added نَ to show multiple people doing the action. This word refers to the false gods (ءَالِهَتُهُمُ) mentioned in the ayah.
Basic form of the particle - this word has no root since it's a primitive particle
Added kasra (ِ) to help with pronunciation. In this ayah, مِن connects the word دُونِ (besides/other than) with شَىْءٍ (thing), showing that their gods could not help them at all.
This is a preposition that shows something is less than, beneath, or other than something else
Adds a damma mark to show this is a fixed preposition
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to Allah (اللَّهِ) that comes after it, showing it means 'besides Allah' or 'other than Allah'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'deity' or 'god'
Changed from the general word for 'a god' to the special name 'Allah' by adding ال and joining letters together
Added kasra at the end to show possession. This word is possessed by دُونِ (besides/other than) in this ayah.
This is a basic preposition that shows separation or distance. In this ayah, it's used with دُونِ to mean 'besides' or 'other than' when talking about false gods.
Root letters ش-ي-أ are joined together with vowels to make the common word for 'a thing'
Added the two dots below (tanween kasra) to show this is 'of a thing' - it connects to the word من before it
This is a word that shows when something happens. In this ayah, it connects two events: when something happened, something else followed.
The doubled ل (shown by ّ) makes the word stronger, like saying 'exactly when' or 'precisely when'
Connected the root letters ج-ي-أ with the vowel marks َ (fatha) to make the past tense verb 'he came'
Added ا (alif) after ج to make it a hollow verb (has a weak middle letter)
The alif is written with madda (آ) to show a longer sound, which adds emphasis in this context
Changed verb into noun meaning 'command' or 'matter'. This word is owned by رَبِّكَ (your Lord) in this ayah.
The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures in a complete and perfect way

steps.m: one who nurtures completely (possessed by أَمْرُ)
steps.n: The kasra (ِ) shows this word belongs to or is connected to أَمْرُ (command/decree)
Added ك at the end to show this refers to your Lord. This whole phrase أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ means 'the command of your Lord'
Basic particle ما that can mean 'what' (for questions) or 'not' (for negation). Here it negates the verb ظَلَمْنٰهُمْ (we wronged them), making it mean 'we did not wrong them'
Added و (and) at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
The middle letter ي changes to ا when making past tense. This is a common pattern with middle-weak verbs.
Making it plural masculine
Added هُمْ to show who received the action (the object). This connects to the previous part of the ayah talking about their false gods.
Basic noun form showing something that is different or other than something else

steps.m: other than (object of زَادُوهُمْ - they increased them)
steps.n: The fatha at the end shows this word is receiving the action of the verb زَادُوهُمْ (they increased them). The full meaning in context is that they increased them in nothing other than destruction.
Added vowels and doubled middle letter to make basic past tense verb
Changed from verb to noun showing repeated/complete destruction
Added tanween kasra to show this is indefinite and possessed by the word غَيْرَ (meaning 'other than')
Starting with the demonstrative particle ذا which means 'that'. The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) in this form.
Added ل with kasra to add emphasis to the demonstrative
Added ك at the end to show we're addressing someone specific
Added ك at the beginning to show comparison or similarity
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: seizing (the seizing of رَبِّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun that means the action itself. This word describes the action of seizing by your Lord mentioned in this ayah.

steps.m: to nurture and care for completely

steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this is the ultimate form of nurturing, making it mean 'Lord' - the one who nurtures and cares for everything completely
Adding kasra (the small line under) shows possession - like saying 'my Lord' or 'your Lord'. It connects to أَخْذُ to mean 'seizure of your Lord'
Adding كَ shows this is your Lord. The word is owned by أَخْذُ (seizure/punishment) in the phrase 'seizure of your Lord', meaning 'your Lord's punishment'
This is a conditional particle that introduces a time condition. It connects with the verb أَخَذَ (took) to show when something happened.
The alif at the end is written as an elongated alif (آ). This is just a spelling variation that doesn't change the meaning.

steps.m: to take or seize something firmly
he took/seized (referring to how your Lord seizes cities)
steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. In this ayah, it's talking about how Allah takes/seizes cities that are wrongdoing.

steps.m: to settle, dwell, or gather in a place

steps.n: Root letters got vowel marks to make a noun meaning 'a place where people live'
Added ال to make it specific/definite, like saying 'the village' instead of just 'a village'
Adds an alif maqsurah (ى) to complete the noun pattern
This combines the conjunction 'and' with the pronoun 'she/it'

steps.m: to be unfair or treat badly

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past
Changed the word to describe someone who does this action
Added ة to make it describe a feminine thing (in this case, it's describing القُرَىٰ which means 'the towns')
Added ٌ to show this is describing القُرَىٰ (the towns) in the verse
Adding the shadda (ّ) on the ن changes this from a basic word meaning 'if/that' into a strong emphasis word. It makes what comes after it more certain and important.
Changed the verb into a noun showing the act of seizing. In this ayah, it refers specifically to Allah's punishment of wrongdoing towns.
Added هُ (his) to show this is Allah's punishment. The َ (fatha) before هُ shows this word is affected by إِنَّ (indeed) earlier in the sentence.
Added ۥٓ which are pronunciation helpers that don't change the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changed from a verb (causing pain) to an adjective (painful) by adding ي and changing vowels to show something that causes intense pain
Added double dammah to make the word indefinite (a painful)
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is a quality or characteristic
Added tanween dammah to show this is describing Allah's grip/seizure mentioned earlier in the ayah (أَخْذُ)
This is the basic form of the word that shows a condition

Adding the shadda changes this from a word meaning 'if' to a word that adds emphasis to what follows. In this ayah, it emphasizes that there is definitely a sign in something for those who fear the punishment of the Hereafter.
This shows where something is located or contained
The ي at the end is written as ى when it comes at the end of a word. This is just a writing change and doesn't affect the meaning.
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (ٰ) to show we're pointing to something far away instead of nearby

steps.m: that (pointing to something far) (refers to يَوْمٌ - Day)
steps.n: Added لِكَ at the end to strengthen the meaning of pointing to something far away. In this ayah, it's pointing to the Day of Judgment mentioned right after it (يَوْمٌ).

steps.m: to show, appear, or be clear
steps.n: The root contains two weak letters (ي)
Adding ة turns this root into the common noun meaning 'sign'
Adding tanween (double mark) makes it indefinite and shows it's being used as an object
Added لَ at start for emphasis (meaning 'surely/indeed') and ً at end to show it's indefinite (like 'a' in English)
This is a question word that became a conditional word meaning 'whoever'. The sukun at the end shows it's complete.
Added لِ at the beginning to show 'for' or 'to'
The لِ combines with the م, shown by the shadda. This word connects to خَافَ (feared) to show 'for whoever feared'.
The root becomes a noun meaning the feeling of fear
Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it mean 'he feared' in the past tense

The middle letter و changes to ا because it's between two fathas. This is a common change in Arabic that makes the word easier to pronounce. This word is connected to عَذَابَ (punishment) in the ayah - it shows who feared the punishment.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added alif after second root letter to make it into a noun meaning the result of the action. This word is what is being feared in the phrase 'feared punishment'
Added alif after first letter to make it into a noun meaning the one who/which comes last
Added feminine ending to make it refer specifically to the afterlife
Added 'the' at beginning and kasra at end to show it follows another word
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby
The alif is written with a special mark (overbar) to show it points to something far away instead of nearby
The ل with kasra (لِ) is added to make the word stronger when pointing to something specific

steps.m: that (pointing to something far away) (describes يَوْمٌ - Day)
steps.n: The كَ is added at the end to emphasize this points to something far away. In this ayah, it's pointing to يَوْمٌ (Day) that's being described.
Added vowel marks to create the noun 'day' from the basic concept of time passing
Added double-damma to show this is the subject being described as مَّشْهُودٌ (witnessed) in this ayah
Added vowels to make it a past tense verb
Changed to show something that was gathered rather than someone doing the gathering
The shadda adds emphasis while the tanween makes it indefinite
Starting with the basic particle ل that shows direction or purpose
The shadda (ّ) appears because this word comes after a word ending in ن or ل in speech, making the sound stronger
Added هُ at the end to show who the word is referring to (him/it)

steps.m: to be social or friendly with others
people (those who are social by nature)
steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun describing people who have this quality naturally
Added emphasis by doubling the first letter
Added ال to point to specific people rather than just any people
Added ُ to show these people are doing something in the sentence
The basic demonstrative pronoun ذا (this) is modified with an alif above (ذٰ) to point to something far away instead of close by
Added لِ to emphasize pointing to something distant
Added كَ at the end to make it a complete word meaning 'that over there' - كَ helps show distance
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Connected the root letters together. The middle letter و stays as a consonant here and isn't changed despite being a weak letter. The fatha on first letter shows this is a basic noun.
The double damma at the end shows this is the subject of the sentence. In this ayah, it's describing a day that is مَّجْمُوعٌ (gathered).
Added مَ at start and made it passive by adding ُو before last letter - changes 'witness' into 'something that is witnessed'
Adding shadda (double letter mark) on م makes the meaning stronger
Adding tanween (double damma) makes the word indefinite
This is a negation particle that makes the following verb negative. In this context, it means 'not' because it comes before the verb نُؤَخِّرُهُ (we delay it)
The و connects this phrase to what came before it, adding 'and' to the meaning
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding shadda to middle root letter creates Form II intensive meaning
The ن at the start shows that 'we' are doing the action
The هُ shows what is being delayed. This connects to وَمَا (and not) at the start of the phrase.
This is a basic exception particle that shows something is different from what was mentioned before. In this ayah, it shows that the delay mentioned (نُؤَخِّرُهُ) only happens for a specific reason.

steps.m: except (connected to نُؤَخِّرُهُ - we delay it)
steps.n: The ا is added at the end to help connect this word smoothly with what comes after it. In this ayah, it connects 'we delay it' with 'for a counted term'.
This root relates to time periods and deadlines
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the noun meaning 'appointed time'
Added the preposition لِ to show purpose or direction - this word appears after إِلَّا (except) in the ayah
Added two dots below (ٍ) to show this word is 'a' term/period (not 'the' term/period) and is affected by إِلَّا before it

steps.m: to count or keep track of numbers
steps.n: This root is about counting and numbers
Basic vowels (fatha) added to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he counted'

steps.m: counted (something that was counted by someone)
steps.n: Changed to show this is something that was counted, not someone doing the counting
Added emphasis to م and added tanwin kasra at end to show this describes the word أَجَلٍ (term/period) in the ayah
Connected the letters together with vowel marks to make the basic noun 'day'
The fatha at the end shows this word describes when something happens. It's connected to the verb يَأْتِ (comes/arrives) in this ayah.
Changes root أتي into present tense 'he comes' by adding يَ at start and vowels in middle

steps.m: he comes/arrives (after يَوْمَ - on the day)
steps.n: The last letter is dropped because it comes after the word يَوْمَ (day), but the meaning stays the same - it still means 'he comes'
This is a negative particle that makes the verb تَكَلَّمُ (speaks) into 'does not speak' in this ayah
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Doubling the middle letter shows doing something with more effort or repeatedly
Adding تَ shows someone doing the action to or for themselves

steps.m: speaks (subject of لَا in this ayah)
steps.n: Adding ُ shows this word is the subject (doer) of the negative particle لَا (not) in this verse
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to create a noun meaning the essence of life - a soul. This word is doing the action of speaking (تَكَلَّمُ) in this verse.
Added the tanween damma (ٌ) to show this refers to any soul in general, not a specific one. It also shows this word is doing the action in the sentence.
This is the basic form of the exception particle that means 'except'
Adding shadda emphasizes the meaning

Adding alif at the end helps connect this word to what follows it in the ayah. Here it introduces an exception to the previous statement, showing that no soul will speak except with Allah's permission.
Changed the verb into a noun that means the act itself
Added هِ to show possession - this belongs to the soul/person mentioned in the ayah (نَفْسٌ). The ِ before هِ shows this word follows the preposition بِ.
Added بِ at the beginning to show this is done 'by means of' permission. The small ۦ at the end is just a pronunciation mark.
This is a basic preposition showing something comes from or is part of something else
Added هُمْ at the end to show we're talking about 'them'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from what came before. In this ayah, it connects to how some people will be miserable while others will be happy.

steps.m: to be miserable or suffer hardship

steps.n: Added vowels to make it mean 'he became' in the past. The و at the end becomes ي when we add vowels.
Added shadda to show this is a strong, lasting quality

steps.m: a wretched one (describing some of the people on Judgment Day)
steps.n: Added tanween dammah to show this describes some unspecified people. This word appears in a verse about the Day of Judgment, describing the state of some people on that day.

steps.m: fortunate/happy (contrasts with شَقِىٌّ - miserable in this ayah)
steps.n: Forms an adjective describing someone characterized by happiness/good fortune. This word is paired as an opposite to شَقِىٌّ (miserable) in this ayah.

steps.m: a fortunate one (one of two types of people mentioned)
steps.n: The tanween (ٌ) shows this describes one of two types of people being discussed (the fortunate ones vs. the miserable ones)

steps.m: and a fortunate one (contrasting with the miserable ones)
steps.n: The وَ (and) connects this to the previous description of شَقِىٌّ (miserable ones), showing these are the two types of people being discussed
This is a particle that introduces a new topic or condition. It's used to focus attention on what comes next, like saying 'Now, regarding...' in English

steps.m: then as for (introduces the first part of ٱلَّذِينَ شَقُوا۟)
steps.n: The فَ shows this starts a new section after previous information. In this ayah, it introduces the first group being discussed (those who are wretched), before another group is mentioned later.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Adding ال makes it specifically point to someone or something being described
Adding ين makes it refer to multiple people. This word describes the people who are mentioned in شَقُوا (they were wretched)
Adding the proper pronunciation marks and changing ا to ٱ for Quranic spelling
This is the basic concept of being miserable or wretched
Added vowels to make the past tense verb for one person
Changed the ending to show multiple people did the action. The ي changes to و because it's a weak letter at the end.
This is a helper word that shows something is inside or within something else, like saying 'the book is in the bag' in English

steps.m: then in (connects to شَقُوا۟ from previous part)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this follows from the previous word شَقُوا۟ (they are wretched), like saying 'they are wretched, then in...'
Changed the final ي to ى which is just a writing style change - it means the same thing
Changes from basic root to noun meaning 'fire'
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ن with shadda
Added kasra at the end because it follows the word فِى (in). Changed initial alif to wasl form since it's standard in writing.
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the letter lam

steps.m: for/to them (referring to those who are wretched in النَّارِ)
steps.n: Added the pronoun هم with damma and sukun to show it refers to a group being talked about. This connects to the people mentioned as شَقُوا (wretched) who are in النَّارِ (the Fire).
Added the kasra vowel mark to show the correct pronunciation

steps.m: in it (referring to النَّارِ - the Fire)
steps.n: Added the feminine pronoun ها with fatha to show this means 'in it', referring back to 'the Fire' (النَّارِ) which is feminine in Arabic

steps.m: to breathe out heavily with a sound

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past-tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun describing the sound itself

steps.m: a heavy breathing sound (of those in Hell-fire)
steps.n: Added tanween to show this is one instance of the sound, specifically referring to the sounds made by those in Hell-fire as mentioned in the ayah

steps.m: to gasp deeply (especially when crying or in distress)

steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters to show this is an intense, recurring sound. In this ayah, it describes one of the terrible sounds made by those in Hellfire.
The tanween shows this is one of multiple sounds. This word is paired with زَفِيرٌ (exhaling) in the ayah to describe the complete distressed breathing cycle.

steps.m: and a deep gasping (paired with زَفِيرٌ)
steps.n: The و connects this to زَفِيرٌ (exhaling) to show these are two parts of the same distressed breathing in Hell
Added vowels to connect the root letters and make it a past tense verb
Added alif after first letter and changed middle vowel to kasra to make it describe someone who does the action
Changed to plural by adding ين at the end to show multiple people staying/remaining. The alif changed shape but still makes the same sound.
Added a small diagonal line (fatha) at the end to show this word is the object (something being acted upon)
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment. It's one of the most common Arabic prepositions.
Added the kasra vowel mark to show how the word is pronounced

steps.m: in it/her (referring to السَّمٰوٰتُ - the heavens)
steps.n: Added ها which refers back to السَّمٰوٰتُ (the heavens) mentioned in the ayah. This shows who or what is being stayed in.
This is a special word that connects time periods. Here it works with دَامَتِ (they remain) to mean 'as long as' or 'for the duration that' something continues.

steps.m: to continue or remain in a state

steps.n: Added short vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'he continued' in the past
The middle letter و becomes ا, which is a common change for hollow verbs
Added تِ at the end to show this is about something feminine (the heavens) continuing
Root meaning 'rise up' becomes 'sky' by adding اء, since the sky is high above us
Added ات to show there are many heavens, with و added to connect the sounds
Added ٱل to point to specific heavens, and doubled the س (shown by ّ)
Added ُ to show this is the subject doing the action in دَامَتِ (remained/lasted). Also changed the long aa sounds to special long aa marks (ٰ)
Added vowel marks to connect the letters into a basic noun
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and ُ to show it's the subject doing the action of continuing/remaining
Added وَ to connect this with the previous word ٱلسَّمٰوٰتُ (the heavens)
This is the basic form of the exceptive particle that excludes something from what was mentioned before
Adding shadda makes the 'l' sound stronger, emphasizing the exclusion

steps.m: except (connects to مَا شَآءَ رَبُّكَ - what your Lord wills)
steps.n: The alif at the end helps connect this word to what follows it. In this ayah, it introduces an exception to how long people will stay - they'll stay forever, except for what Allah wills.

steps.m: as long as (دَامَتِ the heavens and earth remain)
steps.n: In this ayah, مَا is used with دَامَتِ (remained/lasted) to mean 'as long as' - showing how long something will last. It's connecting the time of staying in paradise to how long the heavens and earth remain.
Added vowel marks (fatha) to make it a past tense verb meaning 'he wanted'
Changed to a hollow verb form by adding long alif between first and second root letters

steps.m: to nurture, care for, and have authority over

steps.n: Connected the letters and doubled the middle letter with shadda to show intensity of meaning
Added ُ to show this is the subject, and ك at the end means 'your' when talking to one person. This word is the subject of فَعَّالٌ (effecter/doer) in this ayah.
This word makes what comes after it stronger and more certain. In this ayah, it emphasizes that 'your Lord' (رَبَّكَ) is truly 'effecter' (فَعَّالٌ) of what He intends.
The shadda combines the repeated ب letter to show intensity, making it mean 'one who nurtures completely'

steps.m: your Lord (being acted upon in إِلَّا مَا شَآءَ)
steps.n: Adds كَ to show it belongs to 'you'. This word is being acted upon by شَآءَ (willed/wished) in the phrase 'except what your Lord willed'
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who does intensely/repeatedly (subject of يُرِيدُ)
steps.n: The doubled ع and added alif create a pattern that shows someone who does something intensely or as their main characteristic

steps.m: one who does intensely/repeatedly (subject of يُرِيدُ)
steps.n: The double line (tanween) on top shows this word is complete on its own and is doing the action
This combines two parts: لِ meaning 'for/to' and مَا meaning 'what'. Together they connect the word يُرِيدُ (he wills) to what comes before it.

steps.m: surely for what (connects to يُرِيدُ - he wills)
steps.n: The shadda (ّ) on the ل adds emphasis, showing this is definitely or surely happening
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the past tense form
Added يُ at the start to make it present tense. The middle letter و changed to ي to make it easier to say.
Added ُ at the end to show it's a complete present tense statement
This is a basic particle used to introduce a new topic or condition. It's like saying 'Speaking about...' in English.
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this to the previous statement, showing a contrast or transition.
Added ال to make it specific. The shadda on ل shows it combines with the following letter.

steps.m: those who (subject of سُعِدُوا۟ - they were made happy)
steps.n: Added ين at the end to show we're talking about multiple people. This word refers to the people who were made happy, as mentioned in the next word سُعِدُوا۟.

steps.m: was made happy (passive of يُسْعِدُ - he makes happy)
steps.n: Changed to passive form to show someone else caused the happiness. This word shows the people didn't make themselves happy - Allah made them happy.

steps.m: they were made happy (referring to believers in الَّذِينَ)
steps.n: Added plural masculine ending to show it's talking about a group. This refers back to 'those who' (الَّذِينَ) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Basic preposition showing location or being inside something. Here it shows being in Paradise (الْجَنَّةِ).

steps.m: then/so in (showing result of being happy in الْجَنَّةِ)
steps.n: Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result of what came before - the happy people mentioned will therefore be in Paradise.
Added vowels and doubled the middle letter to show complete covering

steps.m: a garden (a place completely covered with vegetation)
steps.n: The ة makes it a noun for a place characterized by complete covering with vegetation
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific garden - in this case Paradise itself
The kasra shows this follows فِى (in) from earlier in the verse, describing where the blessed people (سُعِدُوا۟) will be
Added vowels to make it 'he' past tense, meaning he stayed forever
Changed to active participle form to show who does the action
Makes the word plural - means more than one person who remains forever

steps.m: those who remain forever (in accusative case)
steps.n: Adds fatha mark to show this is the object of a verb or preposition
Added the kasra vowel mark to show how the word is pronounced
Added ها at the end to show what is being referred to. Here it refers back to Paradise (الْجَنَّةِ) mentioned earlier in the verse.
This is a special word that connects time periods. Here it works with the verb دَامَتِ (they remain/last) to mean 'as long as they last' referring to the heavens and earth.
Added fatha marks and changed و to ا because it's in the middle. This makes it past tense.
Added ت to show this is about something feminine (the heavens/السَّمٰوٰتُ in this ayah)
Added kasra at the end because this word connects to what comes after it
Added vowels to make a basic past-tense verb
The verb 'to rise up' became a noun meaning 'sky' because the sky is high up above us
Made plural form to show multiple heavens
Added 'the' and doubled the س to show it's specific heavens being discussed
Changed to show this word is the subject (doer) by adding ُ at the end, with a slight spelling change
Connected the root letters together to form the basic noun
Added damma to show this is doing something in the sentence (subject)
Added ال to make it specific - we're talking about THE earth

steps.m: and the earth (as the subject of دَامَتِ)
steps.n: Added و to connect this with السَّمٰوٰتُ (the heavens) mentioned before
Starting with the basic conditional particle إِنْ which is one of the components that formed this word
Added the negation particle لَا to create the combined meaning

steps.m: except/unless (excluding what comes after from what was mentioned before)
steps.n: The two words merged over time, with the ن being assimilated into the ل with a shadda, creating a new meaning of exception

steps.m: as long as (دَامَتِ the heavens and earth remain)
steps.n: This is a special word that connects time-related ideas. Here it works with دَامَتِ to mean 'as long as' something continues.
Added vowels (fatha marks) to make it past tense 'he wanted' - like adding -ed in English. The root letters stay the same but the vowels show it's past tense.
The alif (ا) is added after the first letter, which is common with hollow verbs (verbs with و or ي in middle). The meaning stays the same.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, raise up

steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this an intensive form, emphasizing the complete nature of the nurturing
Changes to show this word is doing the action (like saying 'the Lord' vs just 'Lord')

steps.m: your Lord (referring to Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)
steps.n: Adds كَ at the end to show this refers to 'your Lord' when speaking to one person (in this case, Prophet Muhammad ﷺ)

steps.m: to give or present something to someone

steps.n: Changed from root meaning 'to give' into a verbal noun meaning 'giving' by adding the vowels a-a-aa
Added ء at the end to make it a complete noun meaning 'a gift'
Added tanween fatHa to show this is the object being acted upon in the sentence

steps.m: to change or be different from something

steps.n: Added fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to make it a noun meaning 'other than'
Added fatha at the end because this word is describing عَطَآءً (a gift) in the ayah

steps.m: to cut or break into pieces

steps.n: Added short 'a' vowels (fatha) to make it mean 'he cut' in the past
Changed from 'to cut' into 'something that was cut off' - like changing 'break' to 'broken'

steps.m: cut off/interrupted (describing something owned by or connected to something else)
steps.n: The tanween kasra shows this describes something that belongs to or is connected to something else in the sentence
This is the basic negative particle لا that means 'not'
Added فَ at the beginning to show this is a result or sequence. This connects to the verb تَكُ (be) that comes after it, telling the Prophet not to be in doubt.
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Changed to present tense 'you' form by adding تَ at start and changing vowels
The ون is dropped because this word follows فَلَا (so do not). This is a common shortening in Arabic.
Basic preposition showing location or position within something. Here it connects 'doubt' (مِرْيَةٍ) with what these people worship.
The ي at the end is written as ى when it's at the end of a word. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Changed the root into a noun by adding specific vowel patterns
Added ة to show one specific instance of doubting

steps.m: any doubt (in فَلَا تَكُ فِى - so do not be in)
steps.n: Added the genitive case ending because it comes after فِى (in). The tanwin (ٍ) shows it's indefinite - meaning any doubt, not a specific doubt.
Starting with the two separate words: مِنْ meaning 'from' and مَا meaning 'what'

steps.m: from what (referring to what يَعْبُدُ these people worship)
steps.n: When مِنْ comes before مَا, the ن merges into the م, shown by the شدّة (doubled letter mark). This is a common pronunciation rule in Arabic when ن meets certain letters.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present tense, changed vowels to show ongoing action
This is the basic pointing word that shows something is near to the speaker
The alif is written as a small mark above the ه to show it's the same word but written differently
Added a small mark (ٓ) to show this is a complete word part
Added ؤُلَآ to show we're pointing to multiple things/people
Added ءِ at the end to complete the word. This word is the subject (doer) of يَعْبُدُونَ (they worship) in this ayah.

steps.m: that which (object of يَعْبُدُ - they worship)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that connects to the verb يَعْبُدُ, showing what is being worshipped
Basic meaning of serving or worshipping someone/something
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they worship (referring to the disbelievers mentioned as هٰٓؤُلَآءِ)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action. This refers to the disbelievers mentioned as 'these people' (هٰٓؤُلَآءِ) in the ayah.
This is a basic particle used to show exception - it tells us that something is different from or not included in what was mentioned before

steps.m: except/only (exception to يَعْبُدُ - what they worship)
steps.n: Adding the shadda and alif makes the exception stronger and clearer. In this ayah, it's showing an exception to how they worship, connecting to the verb يَعْبُدُ (they worship)
Basic comparison particle that shows similarity

steps.m: just as/like what (refers to how يَعْبُدُ - they worship)
steps.n: Added مَا which makes the comparison more general and refers to the way of worship mentioned in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create past tense verb

steps.m: he worships (referring to what هٰٓؤُلَآءِ/these people worship)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by: 1) adding يَ at start to show 'he', 2) putting sukun on ع to help pronunciation, 3) changing middle vowel to damma, and 4) adding damma at end to show it's happening now. This word describes what 'these people' (هٰٓؤُلَآءِ) are doing in terms of worship.

steps.m: to be a father or have fatherly qualities
steps.n: Root related to fatherhood
Combined root letters to form basic singular noun
Changed to plural form using special pattern for important family terms
The word 'fathers' gets a waaw with damma at the end to connect with what comes after it

steps.m: their fathers (referring to the disbelievers mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added pronoun 'their' to show possession
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'from'
The shadda appears because this word connects with the following word مَّا, causing the 'n' sound to merge with the 'm' sound that follows it
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters and form a noun meaning 'before'
Added damma (U sound) at the end to show this word is in nominative case
This is an emphasis word that makes what comes after it stronger, like saying 'truly' or 'certainly'
Added و at the beginning to connect this part to what came before it
Added ا at the end to show 'we' are doing the action
Root meaning related to completing or fulfilling something
Adds مُ at start to mean 'one who' and doubles the middle letter to show doing something completely
Adds و at the end to make it plural (more than one fulfiller)
The suffix هم indicates that the fulfillment belongs to them (masculine plural, third person).  It shows possession.

steps.m: We will certainly give them in full
steps.n: The لَ prefix adds emphasis and combines with context to show future certainty

steps.m: to set up or assign a share
steps.n: Root related to setting up or establishing something
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create basic past tense verb
Changes from verb 'to set up' to noun meaning 'a portion' by adding ي and kasra
Added هُمْ at the end to show the share belongs to them
Connected the letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create a noun meaning 'other than'
Added fatha at the end because this word is describing نَصِيبَهُمْ (their portion) which is in the accusative case
Root related to reduction or decrease
Added vowels to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Changed to show something that received the action of being decreased

steps.m: something that has been decreased (describing نَصِيبَهُمْ/their portion)
steps.n: Added the tanween kasra to show this word describes 'their portion' (نَصِيبَهُمْ) in the ayah
This is the base particle that shows certainty or completion of an action
Added ل at the beginning to add emphasis, like saying 'surely' instead of just 'indeed'
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Changed to causative form - makes the meaning 'to make something come' which becomes 'to give'
Changed ى to ي with sukun to prepare for suffix
Added نَا at the end to show 'we' did the action. This word connects to مُوسَى (Moses) who received what was given.
This is a proper noun borrowed from Hebrew/Egyptian meaning 'drawn out of water'. In this ayah, Moses is the one who was given the book, showing his role as a recipient of divine revelation.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
Changed the verb into a noun by changing the first vowel to kasra (ِ) and adding alif (ا) after the second letter
Added ٱلْ to make it specific ('the book') and fatha at the end to show it's the object being given. This is the book/scripture that was given to Moses.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added اِ at start and ت after first letter to show people actively disagreeing with each other (form VIII verb pattern)
Changed to passive voice to show the action happened to something rather than someone doing it
Added فَ at beginning to mean 'then/so', and changed spelling of alif to ٱ after فَ as is common
Basic preposition meaning 'in' or 'within'

steps.m: in it (referring to الْكِتٰبَ - the Book)
steps.n: Added the pronoun ه (him/it) to show what is being referred to. Here it refers back to the Book (الْكِتٰبَ) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Added vowel marks to show correct pronunciation of this conditional particle that means 'if not for' or 'were it not for'

steps.m: and if not for (connects to previous statement)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this condition with the previous statement, showing these ideas are linked together
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to create the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he spoke' (verb) to 'a word' (noun) by adding ة and changing middle vowel
Adding tanween makes the noun indefinite. This word is the subject (doer) of the verb سَبَقَتْ (preceded), so it takes the nominative case.
Added fathas to connect the letters and make it past tense. This word describes what كَلِمَةٌ (a word/decree) did.
Added ت with sukun at the end to show this is about كَلِمَةٌ (a feminine word meaning 'word/decree')
This is a basic preposition showing where something comes from or its source. In this ayah, it connects the word شَكٍّ (doubt) with هُ (it/him), showing they are in doubt from/about it.
Added fatha and shadda to create the noun meaning 'lord' - someone who takes care of others
Added kasra to show this word belongs to or is connected to something
Added كَ at the end to show this means 'your Lord'
Added shadda on first letter for extra emphasis, showing the importance of the word
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The vowel pattern changes to show this happened to something rather than something doing it

steps.m: surely it would have been decided (in response to لَوْلَا - if not for)
steps.n: Added لَ to show this is a response to 'if not for' (لَوْلَا) earlier in the verse. It shows what would have definitely happened if something else hadn't prevented it.

steps.m: to be separate or distinct from something else
steps.n: This root became a preposition that shows something is in the middle of other things
Adding vowels turns this root into the common preposition meaning 'between'

steps.m: between them (referring to those who disagreed about the Book)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show this refers to the people who differed about the Book mentioned earlier in the verse
This is a basic emphasis word that makes statements stronger
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it
Added هُمْ at the end to show who is being talked about (they/them)
Basic preposition showing location or presence within something
Added لَ at the beginning to add emphasis. This word connects to شَكٍّ (doubt) in the ayah, showing they are 'surely in doubt'.
The doubled middle letter makes the meaning stronger
Added tanween kasra to show this is indefinite and is being acted upon by the preposition في in the phrase لَفِى شَكٍّ (indeed in doubt)
Basic preposition meaning 'from'
The n sound blends into the next letter, making it stronger (shown by the shadda)
Adding هُ shows this means 'from it', referring back to the doubt (شَكٍّ) mentioned in the ayah
Connected the root letters with basic vowels to form the noun for 'doubt'

steps.m: causing doubt (describes something that makes others doubt)

steps.n: This word describes شَكٍّ (doubt) in the phrase شَكٍّ مُرِيبٍ (a doubt that causes more doubt)
This is a special word that makes statements stronger. It tells us to pay extra attention to what comes next because it's important.
Added وَ (wa) at the beginning to connect this statement with what came before it, like saying 'and' in English
The doubled ل (shadda) shows completeness, while the damma makes it a noun. This word is what will receive the complete reward mentioned in لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ (He will surely give them in full).

steps.m: all of them (as the object receiving complete reward)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word is receiving the action of لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ (He will surely give them in full). It refers to everyone getting their complete reward.
This is a special word used to talk about timing of events. The shadda on م shows emphasis.
The doubled ل adds extra emphasis and connects this word to لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ (surely He will repay them) that comes after it, showing a strong connection between the timing and what will happen.

steps.m: to fulfill completely or pay in full

steps.n: Added يُ at the beginning to show present/future tense and doubled the middle letter (ف) to show doing something thoroughly. This verb refers to رَبُّكَ (your Lord) who is doing the action.
Added لَ at the beginning to emphasize that this will definitely happen
Added نَّ at the end as a second emphasis marker, making the certainty even stronger

steps.m: He (your Lord) will most definitely pay them in full (their أَعْمٰلَهُمْ/deeds)
steps.n: Added هُمْ to show who receives the action - it refers to everyone getting their full reward/recompense for their deeds (أَعْمٰلَهُمْ)
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a basic past tense verb
The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this a noun for someone who does the action perfectly and completely

steps.m: one who cares for completely (as the doer)
steps.n: The word gets a damma (u sound) on top to show it's doing the action

steps.m: your complete caretaker (as the doer of لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ - will give them in full)
steps.n: Adding كَ (ka) at the end makes it 'your Lord' instead of just 'Lord'. This Lord is the one who will give them their reward.

steps.m: to work, to act, to do deeds

steps.n: Adding the three fathas makes it a complete past tense verb
Changes from a single action/deed to many deeds by making it plural using the أَفْعَال pattern - a common way to make plurals in Arabic
Added هُمْ suffix with fatha connector to show possession (their deeds). This word is the object of لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ (He will surely give them in full) in this ayah.

steps.m: indeed (emphasizes لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ - 'He will surely give them in full')
steps.n: This word emphasizes the promise that comes after it in the ayah. It's like adding an exclamation mark or saying 'definitely' in English.

steps.m: indeed He (refers to رَبُّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Added هُۥ which means 'He' and refers back to 'your Lord' mentioned earlier in the ayah. The small و (ۥ) after هُ is just to help with pronunciation.
This is a connecting word that links to the verb يَعْمَلُونَ (they do/work). It refers to the actions or deeds being done.
Added بِ to show how something is done or the means by which it happens. Here it connects to خَبِيرٌ (All-Aware) to show Allah is aware by/through what they do.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start, putting sukun on middle letter, and damma at end
Added ون to show multiple people doing the action. This word is the subject of لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ (He will surely give them in full) in this ayah.

steps.m: one who is deeply aware (of يَعْمَلُونَ)
steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters to show someone who has this quality very strongly. This word describes Allah's complete awareness of what they do (يَعْمَلُونَ) in this ayah.
The tanween shows this is one of Allah's attributes mentioned generally, not referring to a specific instance of awareness
Added vowels to connect the root letters
Added است at the beginning to show trying to do something
Changed from regular verb to command form, dropping the weak letter و to make it easier to say
Added فَ at start to mean 'so' or 'then', connecting it to previous statements
Starting with the comparison particle كَ which shows similarity
Added ما which makes the comparison more general, showing 'whatever way' or 'as'

steps.m: just as (connected to أُمِرْتَ - you were commanded)
steps.n: Extended the alif for emphasis. This word connects to أُمِرْتَ (you were commanded), showing that someone should be steadfast exactly as they were commanded to be.

steps.m: to command/order someone to do something

steps.n: Changed from 'to command' to 'was commanded' by adding special vowel marks (damma and kasra) to make it passive. The doer of the action is not mentioned.
Adding 'تَ' shows this happened to 'you' (when speaking to one male). This word follows كَمَآ (just as) in the ayah.
This is a basic particle that refers to people in a general way, like saying 'whoever' or 'anyone who' in English
Added وَ at the beginning to connect this with the previous phrase. In this ayah, it refers to whoever repented along with the Prophet.

steps.m: to return back from wrong to right
he returned back (from wrong to right)
steps.n: Basic vowels (fatha) added to make it 'he returned' in past tense
The weak letter و changes to ا, which is a common pattern for hollow verbs in past tense
This is the base word meaning 'with'. It's a particle (حرف) that doesn't come from a root.
Added كَ at the end to show this means 'with you'. This connects to the command فَٱسْتَقِمْ (so be steadfast) in the ayah, telling who should be steadfast.
This is the basic negation particle that means 'not' or 'do not'

steps.m: and not (connected to فَٱسْتَقِمْ - so be steadfast)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this prohibition with the previous command. This links 'do not transgress' with 'be steadfast' in the ayah.
Changed from basic root to past tense verb by adding vowels (َ) and writing ى at the end to show 'he' did the action
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' and changed middle vowel to sukun for present tense
Added وْا at the end to show multiple people are being addressed. The small circle above shows this is a command not to do something.
Adding shadda changes this from 'if' to 'indeed', making it an emphasis particle

steps.m: indeed he/it (referring to بِمَا تَعْمَلُونَ - what you do)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who/what is being referred to. Here it refers back to 'what you do' later in the ayah
Added a small و mark (ۥ) after هُ to help with pronunciation. This doesn't change the meaning
This is a relative pronoun that connects to the verb تَعْمَلُونَ (you do/are doing), showing 'what you do'
Added بِ at the beginning to show that Allah sees 'by/with' what you do. This connects to بَصِيرٌ (All-Seeing) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The تَ at the start shows 'you' are doing the action now, not in the past

steps.m: you (all) do (referring to those who repented in وَمَن تَابَ)
steps.n: Adding ون makes it refer to multiple people. In this ayah, it refers to the group of people who have repented, mentioned earlier with وَمَن تَابَ (and whoever repented)

steps.m: one who sees and understands deeply
steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters and vowel marks to create intensive form showing someone who does this action extensively

steps.m: All-Seeing, All-Aware (describing how Allah sees and knows everything) (connected to تَعْمَلُونَ - what you do)
steps.n: Adding ٌ shows this is describing Allah's attribute of complete awareness and insight into everything people do
This is a negative particle that tells us the action تَرْكَنُوٓا۟ (inclining) is not allowed
Added 'and' to connect this prohibition to previous instructions
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense verb
Adding damma at end shows this is in regular/default case

steps.m: you all lean/rely (towards الَّذِينَ ظَلَمُوا۟ - those who do wrong)
steps.n: Added وا at the end to emphasize this is addressing a group. In this ayah, it's warning against leaning towards or relying upon those who do wrong.
This is a preposition that shows direction or movement towards something. In this ayah, it connects 'do not incline' to 'those who', showing who you shouldn't incline towards.
This word points to a group of people and connects them to what comes next

steps.m: to do wrong, to act unjustly against someone

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make the past tense verb. This word describes who you should not incline towards (تَرْكَنُوٓا۟) in this ayah.
Added وا with damma before it to show multiple people (they) did the action. These are the wrongdoers that the ayah warns not to incline towards.

steps.m: to touch or feel something physically

steps.n: Added vowels and doubled the middle letter (shown by shadda) to make 'he touched'
Added تَ at beginning to show something happening to itself, with extra intensity in the meaning
Added كُمُ to show the action affects multiple people

steps.m: then it will touch you all intensely (consequence of رَكَنُوا/inclining)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this is a result of the previous action (inclining towards wrongdoers)
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make it pronounceable
The weak letter و changes to ا, making this word specifically mean 'fire' rather than just light
Adds ُ to show this is the subject doing the touching in تَمَسَّكُمُ (touch you)
Adding ٱل makes it 'the fire' specifically. The ن gets doubled (shadda) because it's a 'sun letter' that combines with the ل of ٱل

steps.m: not (in this ayah: negates having protectors besides Allah)
steps.n: This is a negative particle that makes something not true. In this ayah, it's saying you do NOT have protectors besides Allah.

steps.m: and not (in this ayah: and you do not have protectors besides Allah)
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this idea to the previous warning about not inclining towards wrongdoers. It's explaining two related warnings: don't incline to wrongdoers AND don't think you have protectors besides Allah.
This is a particle showing direction or purpose. Here it connects to 'the fire' (ٱلنَّارُ) in the ayah.
Added كُم to show this applies to multiple people being addressed. In this ayah, it's warning that the fire will be for all those who incline towards wrongdoers.
This is a basic particle that shows where something comes from or what it's made of. It's like saying 'from' or 'of' in English.

The م gets a shadda because it comes after a word ending in ن or م. This is just for easier pronunciation - it doesn't change the meaning. Here it connects with the word دُونِ (besides/other than) that comes after it.
The root carries the basic idea of being lower or beneath something
Adding the damma vowel mark makes it into a proper word meaning 'below'
Added kasra at the end to show this connects to the word Allah after it, meaning 'besides Allah' or 'other than Allah'
Connected the letters and added vowels to make the word for 'deity'
Added kasra at end to show this means 'of' a deity

This is a preposition that shows where something comes from or what it belongs to. In this ayah, it connects the word دُونِ (besides/other than) with أَوْلِيَآءَ (protectors/allies), showing you have no protectors from besides Allah.

steps.m: to be close to and protect

steps.n: Adding vowels makes it a past tense verb showing someone protected or was close to something
Changed from 'he protected' to multiple people who protect - like going from guardian to guardians

steps.m: protectors, close allies (object of مِن دُونِ)
steps.n: Added fatha at end to show this word is affected by مِن دُونِ (besides/instead of) in the ayah
This is a conjunction particle (حرف عطف) that shows temporal or logical sequence

steps.m: then, after that (connecting وَلَا تَرْكَنُوٓا۟ with لَا تُنصَرُونَ)
steps.n: Shows this word connects two complete ideas - first 'do not lean towards' then 'you will not be helped'. The marks (ُ and َّ) help show it means 'then' or 'after that'

steps.m: not (negates تُنصَرُونَ - you will be helped)
steps.n: This is a negative particle that tells us the action تُنصَرُونَ (you will be helped) will not happen. It's like adding 'not' before a verb in English.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he helped' to 'you are helped' by adding تُ at start and changing vowels to make it passive present tense
Added ونَ to show it applies to a group of people being helped

steps.m: to establish, set up, make firm

steps.n: Connected the root letters with vowels to form basic past tense verb
Added kasra at the end to mark it as a command
